The morgue's fluorescent lights hummed like dying insects. Liang Yue stood over her grandmother's preserved body, the brass furnace burning cold fire in her dantian. Professor Li had left her alone—a calculated kindness, she understood. Some grief needed no witnesses.

Grandma looked smaller than she remembered. The embalming process had smoothed away the worry lines, the laugh lines, the deep creases between her brows that appeared whenever Liang Yue brought home a mediocre exam grade. She looked peaceful. She looked dead.

But the toxicology report told a different story.

Liang Yue spread the photos across the stainless steel table. The liver cross-section showed dark splotches where healthy tissue should have been—clusters of cellular death that radiated outward like spiderwebs. The chemical marker highlighted something called aconitine, a compound found in *Aconitum carmichaelii*, also known as monkshood or *fuzi*.

Traditional Chinese medicine used processed *fuzi* in small doses to treat pain and inflammation. But the levels in Grandma's system weren't small. They were lethal.

*Someone had been feeding her poison for months.*

Liang Yue's hands clenched. The furnace pulsed, responding to her anger. She could feel it—the way the Qi inside her churned, wanting to break free, wanting to *do* something. But what could she do? She was a pharmacy student with a magical furnace and a dead grandmother.

She was nothing.

"You can take samples if you need." Professor Li's voice came from the doorway. "I've already documented everything, but if you want independent verification—"

"Who?" Liang Yue cut him off. "Who at the Zhang family would do this? And why wait until she was old? Why not just take the furnace when she was younger?"

"Because they couldn't find it." Professor Li stepped closer, his face grave. "Your grandmother was a master of concealment. She hid the furnace's true nature so well that even the Zhang family's best spies couldn't locate it. They thought she'd lost it, or sold it, or that it had been destroyed during the Cultural Revolution."

"But they still killed her."

"To make sure. And to send a message to anyone else who might know about Internal Alchemy." He paused. "They're thorough, the Zhangs. Ruthless. Once they commit to a course of action, they don't stop."

Liang Yue stared at her grandmother's face. "Then why haven't they come for me yet? I've been using the furnace. I've made pills. I've left traces."

"Because they don't know you have it. Not yet." Professor Li's voice dropped. "But they will. The girl you healed—Xiao Mei—her mother works at a Zhang-owned clinic. The news has already spread. People are talking about the miracle medicine that cured a dying child."

Cold spread through Liang Yue's chest. "How long?"

"Three days, maybe less. The Zhangs have informants everywhere. Once they connect the dots—once they realize that a pharmacy student from Sichuan University suddenly has access to pills that shouldn't exist—they'll come."

Liang Yue looked down at the furnace, visible only to her inner sight. It pulsed, warm and alive, like a second heart. *Don't let them take it. Don't let them twist the Way.*

"I need to learn," she said. "I need to get stronger. Faster."

"I know someone who can teach you."

Her head snapped up. "Who?"

"An old friend of your grandmother's. A former martial monk from the Shaolin Temple who left the order seventy years ago. He's been living in the mountains outside Chengdu, surviving on wild herbs and meditation. He might be the last person alive who remembers the old ways."

"He's still alive? He'd be—"

"One hundred and four years old. And stronger than most men half his age." Professor Li pulled out his phone, showing a photograph. An old man, lean and weathered, sitting cross-legged on a cliff edge. His eyes were closed, but there was something about his posture—a stillness that seemed almost supernatural.

"His name is Master Chen. He was your grandmother's teacher."

Liang Yue's breath caught. Grandma had never mentioned a teacher. She'd always implied that she'd learned the Way from her own grandmother, passed down through generations of women. But this—this changed everything.

"Why didn't she tell me?"

"Because she wanted to protect him. The Zhangs have been hunting the last practitioners of Internal Alchemy for decades. They've killed or imprisoned everyone they've found. Master Chen survived only by disappearing completely." Professor Li put away the phone. "But he knows you're coming. I contacted him last night."

"He knows about the furnace?"

"He knows about *you*. That's enough."

Liang Yue looked at her grandmother one last time. The peaceful face, the closed eyes, the hands folded over her chest. *I'll find out who did this, Grandma. I'll make them pay. But first, I need to survive.*

"Take me to him," she said.

---

The drive took four hours, winding through mountain roads that grew narrower and more treacherous with each kilometer. Professor Li drove in silence, his hands steady on the wheel. Liang Yue watched the city lights fade behind them, replaced by darkness and the occasional flicker of fireflies.

By the time they reached the trailhead, the sun had set. The forest swallowed them whole.

"This way." Professor Li led her up a path that barely qualified as a path—just a break in the undergrowth, marked by stones that had been placed decades ago. The air grew cold and damp. Insects buzzed in the darkness. Somewhere in the distance, a bird called out, its voice echoing through the trees.

"Are there tigers?" Liang Yue asked, trying to keep her voice steady.

"Not anymore. But there are snakes. And bears. And things that are harder to name."

"Things?"

"Qi attracts Qi. Your grandmother's furnace has been dormant for years, but now that it's active again, it's like a beacon. The creatures that live in these mountains—the old creatures—they can feel it."

Liang Yue's hand went to her pocket, where she'd hidden a few emergency pills. Nothing that could fight a bear, but the thought was comforting.

They walked for an hour. Two hours. The path grew steeper, the rocks sharper. Liang Yue's legs ached, her lungs burned, but she kept moving. The furnace pulsed in time with her heartbeat, lending her strength she didn't know she had.

Finally, they reached a clearing. A small wooden cabin sat at its center, built into the side of a cliff. Smoke rose from a chimney. Light flickered through a single window.

Master Chen was waiting on the porch.

He looked even older than the photograph—his skin like cracked earth, his eyes clouded with cataracts. But when he turned to face them, Liang Yue felt something shift in the air. The pressure around her increased, like she was standing at the bottom of a deep ocean. The furnace flared, responding to the presence of another cultivator.

"You're early," Master Chen said. His voice was rough, like stones grinding together. "And you've already drawn attention."

"Attention?" Professor Li stepped forward. "We drove through back roads. No one followed us."

"Not human attention." Master Chen's blind eyes fixed on something behind them. "The forest knows. The spirits know. And they've already sent word to those who would harm you."

Liang Yue turned. In the darkness between the trees, she saw shapes moving—shadows that didn't match the branches they fell across, flickers of movement that came from nowhere and everywhere at once.

"What are those?"

"Watchers. Servants of the Zhang family." Master Chen stood, his joints cracking. "They've been tracking you since you crossed into the mountains. They're waiting for me to leave, to give them an opening."

"Can you fight them?"

"I can." He sounded tired. "But not forever. And not while protecting both of you." He turned and walked into the cabin. "Come inside. We have work to do."

The cabin was sparse—a single room with a dirt floor, a fire pit, and a wooden shelf that held a few clay jars. Master Chen motioned for them to sit on the floor, then lowered himself onto a cushion with surprising grace.

"The furnace," he said. "Show me."

Liang Yue hesitated. But something in his voice—the authority of someone who had walked the Way for a century—made her obey. She reached inside herself, pulling the furnace into visibility. It materialized in her hands, brass and warm, its surface etched with characters she still couldn't fully read.

Master Chen reached out, his fingers hovering just above the metal. He didn't touch it, but Liang Yue felt the connection—a pulse of energy that passed between them, like two currents meeting.

"You've awakened it," he said. "But you haven't bonded with it fully. The furnace still sees you as a caretaker, not a master."

"How do I bond with it?"

"You don't. Not through force, not through will. You become worthy of it." He pulled back his hand. "Your grandmother never fully bonded either. She was strong, but she was afraid. The Zhangs hunted her, and that fear never left her blood."

"I'm not afraid of them."

"You should be." His voice was flat. "Fear is what keeps you alive. The Zhangs have resources you can't imagine. They have scientists, soldiers, and something worse."

"Worse?"

"They have a cultivator of their own. A man who sold his soul for power." Master Chen's blind eyes seemed to see through her. "He's the one who killed your grandmother. Not with poison—that was just to weaken her. He killed her with a technique called the Soul Severing Palm. It destroys the spirit from within."

Liang Yue's blood ran cold. "Then how did she hide the furnace? How did she survive long enough to give it to me?"

"Because she was stronger than he knew. And because she had help." Master Chen reached into his robe, pulling out a jade pendant. "She gave me this before she died. It contains a fragment of her Qi—a message she wanted me to deliver to you."

He handed it over. The jade was warm, pulsing with a familiar energy. Liang Yue held it to her chest, and Grandma's voice filled her mind.

*Yue'er. If you're hearing this, then Chen has found you. I'm sorry I couldn't tell you the truth while I was alive. I'm sorry I couldn't protect you. But you're stronger than you know. The furnace chose you for a reason.*

*The Zhangs have been searching for the Key to the Celestial Pill—a recipe that can grant immortality. They think it's a myth. They're wrong. It's real. And it's hidden in the furnace's core.*

*Don't let them find it. Don't let them use it. Destroy it if you must.*

*But first, learn the truth about who you are.*

*Your mother didn't leave you. She was taken.*

*And she's still alive.*

The jade went cold. Liang Yue's hands were shaking. The world seemed to tilt around her, reality cracking at the edges.

"What?" she whispered. "My mother—Grandma told me she died when I was three. A car accident. She told me—"

"She lied to protect you." Master Chen's voice was gentle. "Your mother was a cultivator. A powerful one. The Zhangs captured her because she knew the location of the Celestial Pill recipe. They've been torturing her for information for twenty years."

"But she didn't tell them?"

"No. She's stronger than

title: The Poison in the Blood
content:
Liang Yue stared at the image, the stained liver cells glowing an ominous violet under the microscope's fluorescent markers. The pattern wasn't random—it was deliberate, targeted, like a signature carved into dead tissue.

"This is Wangshui toxin," she said, her voice hollow. "Refined from the gallbladder of river pufferfish. It's a classical alchemical poison—one of the Thirteen Obscures."

Professor Li's eyebrows shot up. "You recognize it?"

"Grandma taught me the Thirteen Obscures when I was twelve." Liang Yue's finger traced the image, never touching it. "She said every Internal Alchemist needed to know how to recognize death before it recognized them. Wangshui toxin is slow, subtle, mimics cardiac failure. It's also nearly impossible to detect unless you're looking for it specifically."

"Which is why the police missed it."

"They didn't miss it. They ignored it." Liang Yue looked up, her eyes hard. "The Zhang family has someone inside the forensics department. Someone who signed off on 'natural causes' without running the standard toxicology panel."

Professor Li rubbed his temples. "You're thinking like a conspiracy theorist."

"I'm thinking like a granddaughter who just found out her grandmother was murdered." Liang Yue closed the folder. "There's a difference."

She stood up, the furnace pulsing warm against her chest. The energy felt different now—angrier, sharper, like a blade being honed. She'd felt it before, in the days after Grandma's death, when she'd been too numb to understand what the heat in her dantian was trying to tell her.

"Where are you going?" Professor Li asked.

"To the morgue. I need to see her."

"The body's been preserved, but—"

"Then I need to touch her." Liang Yue's voice cracked. "I need to feel what the poison left behind."

Professor Li was silent for a long moment. Then he reached into his desk drawer and pulled out a key card, yellowed with age. "Level B3. Room 117. The night guard is a friend—he won't ask questions if you use the service elevator."

Liang Yue took the card. "Thank you."

"Don't thank me yet. The Zhang family has eyes everywhere. If you're caught—"

"I won't be."

She left the office before he could argue further.

---

The university morgue was exactly as sterile and cold as she'd expected. Fluorescent lights hummed overhead, casting everything in a pale green pallor. The air smelled of formaldehyde and bleach and something else—something metallic, like old blood that had been scrubbed away but never truly erased.

Room 117 was at the end of a narrow corridor, the door unmarked except for a small placard reading "RESEARCH SPECIMENS - AUTHORIZED PERSONNEL ONLY."

Liang Yue swiped the key card. The lock clicked open.

Inside, the room was smaller than she'd anticipated. A single steel table dominated the center, covered by a white sheet that outlined the shape of a body beneath it. Rows of specimen jars lined the walls, each containing organs floating in amber solution—hearts, livers, kidneys, all labeled with dates and case numbers.

She approached the table slowly, her heart hammering against her ribs.

*This is Grandma.*

The thought was unreal, impossible. Grandma had always been larger than life—a tiny woman with iron will and hands that could crush medicinal herbs into dust. She'd survived the Cultural Revolution, the famine, the years of silence when alchemy was outlawed and practitioners were executed. She'd buried three husbands, raised two children, and trained a granddaughter in secrets that had been passed down through twenty-seven generations.

And now she was under a sheet in a university morgue, murdered by men who didn't believe in souls.

Liang Yue pulled back the sheet.

Grandma's face was peaceful, almost serene. The mortician had done good work—the wrinkles smoothed, the gray hair arranged neatly around her shoulders. She looked like she was sleeping.

Except for the faint discoloration around her lips. A bluish tinge that no amount of cosmetics could hide.

Liang Yue pressed her palm against Grandma's forehead. The skin was cold, waxy, devoid of the warmth that had always radiated from Grandma's body even in her final days. But Liang Yue wasn't looking for warmth.

She was looking for residue.

Closing her eyes, she let the furnace's energy flow through her arm, into her hand, through her fingertips. The Qi seeped into Grandma's flesh like water into dry soil, searching for the traces of poison that lingered even in death.

*Wangshui toxin,* she thought, visualizing the molecular structure. *Where are you hiding?*

The furnace pulsed. Once. Twice.

Then she felt it.

A network of dark threads, branching through Grandma's liver, her kidneys, her brain. The poison hadn't just attacked her organs—it had woven itself into her meridians, the energy channels that alchemists cultivated over decades. It was an executioner's work, precise and cruel, designed to destroy not just the body but the soul's connection to it.

*Whoever did this knew exactly what they were doing.*

Liang Yue opened her eyes. Her hand was shaking.

"Grandma," she whispered. "I'm sorry I wasn't there. I'm sorry I didn't see it sooner."

She pulled the sheet back up, covering Grandma's face. But as she did, her fingers brushed against something hard—a lump beneath the fabric, near Grandma's left hand.

Liang Yue frowned. She lifted the sheet again.

Grandma's hand was clenched into a fist, the knuckles white even in death. Something metallic glinted between her fingers.

Liang Yue pried the fingers open, gently, carefully. The metal object fell into her palm.

It was a ring. Silver, tarnished with age, engraved with characters so small she had to squint to read them.

*守一.*

Guard the One.

"I don't understand," she murmured, turning the ring over. "I've never seen this before."

A sound behind her. Footsteps, soft and deliberate.

Liang Yue spun around.

A man stood in the doorway. He was tall, broad-shouldered, with a face that seemed carved from granite. His eyes were dark, unreadable, fixed on her with an intensity that made her skin crawl.

He wore a white lab coat over a black suit. A university ID badge hung from his neck, but the photo was too small to make out.

"You're not supposed to be here," he said, his voice flat.

"I have authorization." Liang Yue held up the key card.

The man didn't look at it. "That card belongs to Professor Li. He doesn't have clearance for this room."

"Then how did I get in?"

A pause. The man's eyes narrowed.

"The door was unlocked," he said slowly. "Which means someone wanted you to find something."

Liang Yue's blood ran cold. She looked down at the ring in her hand.

*Guard the One.*

"What is this?" she asked, holding it up.

The man's expression flickered—just for a moment, but long enough for her to see recognition in his eyes.

"Where did you get that?"

"It was in my grandmother's hand."

The man stepped forward, his movements sudden and predatory. Liang Yue stumbled back, her hip hitting the steel table.

"I'll take that," he said, reaching for the ring.

"No."

She shoved the ring into her pocket, but the man was faster. His hand closed around her wrist, iron-strong, and he twisted.

Pain shot up her arm. The ring fell from her grip, clattering across the floor.

"Stupid girl," the man hissed. "You have no idea what you're holding."

"Then tell me."

He laughed—a cold, ugly sound. "Your grandmother was a thief. She stole that ring from the Zhang family forty years ago. They've been looking for it ever since."

"She was an alchemist. She didn't steal anything."

"She was a *traitor*." The man's grip tightened. "She abandoned the Zhang family's service, took their secrets, and disappeared into the countryside. She spent the rest of her life hiding from what she'd done."

Liang Yue's mind raced. Grandma had never mentioned the Zhang family. Never mentioned a ring. Never mentioned being anyone's servant.

"You're lying."

"Am I?" The man pulled her closer, his breath hot against her face. "Then why did she die with this ring in her hand? Why did she leave it for *you* to find?"

"I don't know."

"But you will." He released her wrist, stepping back. "Take the ring. Take it to whoever sent you here. Tell them the Zhang family knows what they're planning."

"I wasn't sent by anyone."

"Of course not." His smile was mocking. "You just happened to break into a secured morgue at midnight to visit a dead woman. Very convincing."

Liang Yue said nothing. She bent down, picked up the ring, and slipped it onto her finger.

It fit perfectly.

The man's eyes widened. For the first time, she saw something like genuine surprise cross his face.

"How—"

"Get out," Liang Yue said, her voice cold. "Before I decide to find out what the Thirteen Obscures feel like when they're used on a living person."

He stared at her for a long moment. Then he turned and walked away, his footsteps echoing down the corridor.

Liang Yue stood alone in the morgue, the ring warm against her skin, the furnace burning in her chest like a second heart.

*Guard the One.*

She didn't know what it meant. But she was going to find out.

And when she did, the Zhang family was going to learn what happened when you killed the last Internal Alchemist's grandmother.

title: Chapter 11
content:
CHAPTER 11: The Dead Speak in Whispers

The chemical marker in Grandma's liver glowed like a constellation of death—tiny blue-black specks clustered around the bile ducts, spreading outward like the roots of a poisonous tree.

"This is a heavy metal stain," Liang Yue said, her pharmacy training kicking in. "But it's not uniform. It looks like—"

"Like something that crystallized," Professor Li finished. "The toxin was designed to deposit in specific organs. The liver, the kidneys, the heart. It creates multiple micro-embolisms that look like natural failure."

Liang Yue's fingers traced the image. "How long did she have?"

"Six months from initial exposure. Maybe eight." He paused. "She knew. The last time I saw her, she told me she'd started seeing golden threads in her vision. That's a classic symptom of heavy metal accumulation in the optic nerve."

"Grandma was an alchemist. She could have purified her own body."

"She tried." Professor Li pulled out another document—a handwritten note in Grandma's script. *The poison binds to Qi itself. Every attempt at purification only spreads it deeper. The furnace cannot heal what it cannot separate.*

Liang Yue read the words three times, each time feeling a new crack form in her chest. Grandma had known she was dying. She'd known for months. And she'd spent those months not fighting for her own life, but preparing for Liang Yue's.

*Don't let them take it. Don't let them twist the Way.*

"What was the source?" Liang Yue's voice came out flat, controlled. "How did they administer it?"

"Her arthritis medication." Professor Li's jaw tightened. "I found the contaminated batch in her bathroom. The Zhang family owns a pharmaceutical subsidiary—Bright Peak Pharmaceuticals. They've been developing a line of traditional medicine supplements. Grandma was a 'quality control consultant.'"

"She was testing their products."

"And they used her own position to kill her."

The room was silent except for the hum of fluorescent lights. Liang Yue felt the furnace pulse in her dantian, hot and insistent, as if it too understood what had been done.

"I need to see her body."

Professor Li nodded slowly. "It's not going to be easy. The morgue is in the basement of the medical school. Security has been increased since—" He stopped.

"Since what?"

"Since someone tried to break in last week. The Zhang family has people everywhere. If they see you—"

"They won't see me." Liang Yue pulled out her phone, opening a map of the campus. "There's a service tunnel that connects the pharmacy building to the medical school. We used it during orientation week to sneak into the anatomy lab."

"That's how you know about it?"

"I'm a pharmacy student. We're very resourceful."

Twenty minutes later, Liang Yue was crawling through a maintenance tunnel that smelled of rust, mold, and decades of forgotten cigarette smoke. The furnace glowed faintly in her chest, casting just enough light to see by. Behind her, she could hear Professor Li's labored breathing.

"I'm too old for this," he muttered.

"You're the one who wanted to come."

"Because you need someone who can talk to the morgue director. He's an old colleague. He'll let us in if I vouch for you."

They emerged into a storage room filled with boxes of expired IV bags and broken hospital beds. The morgue was through a set of double doors, past a security checkpoint that was currently unmanned.

"Shift change," Professor Li whispered. "We have fifteen minutes."

The morgue was cold—not just in temperature, but in atmosphere. The sterile white tiles seemed to absorb sound, and the rows of refrigerated drawers hummed with a low, mechanical drone. Liang Yue's breath misted in front of her face.

"Drawer 47," Professor Li said, pointing to the far wall. "I had her placed in a private storage unit."

Liang Yue walked toward it, each step feeling heavier than the last. She'd seen Grandma's body at the funeral home, dressed in her favorite blue qipao, looking peaceful and empty. But that was before she knew. Before the poison. Before the murder.

Now, the drawer held evidence.

She pulled the handle. The drawer slid open with a hiss of released air. Grandma's face was pale, frozen in an expression of quiet dignity. Her eyes were closed, her hands folded across her chest. She looked smaller than Liang Yue remembered.

"I'm sorry, Grandma," Liang Yue whispered. "I'm so sorry I didn't know."

She reached out and touched Grandma's cheek. The skin was cold, waxy. But as her fingers made contact, the furnace in her dantian exploded with heat.

*Look deeper.*

The voice wasn't audible. It was a vibration, a resonance that seemed to come from the bones themselves. Liang Yue's vision blurred, and for a moment, she saw something impossible.

Golden threads.

They wove through Grandma's body like a web, pulsing with a faint, sickly light. The poison. But there was something else—a darker mass, a clot of Qi that had settled in the center of Grandma's chest. It was moving.

"What the—"

The morgue lights flickered. The temperature dropped.

Professor Li grabbed her arm. "We need to go. Now."

But Liang Yue couldn't look away. The dark mass was growing, spreading, pushing against Grandma's skin from the inside. It was alive.

The drawer slammed shut on its own.

Liang Yue stumbled backward, heart pounding. The furnace was screaming now, hot enough to burn. She could feel it trying to tell her something, but the message was garbled, fragmented, like a radio station caught between frequencies.

"What was that?" she gasped.

"I don't know." Professor Li's face was pale. "But I've seen it before. In the Zhang family's research files. They call it 'Qi anchoring'—a technique to bind a fragment of the cultivator's will to a dead body. A way to extract information from the deceased."

"They're using Grandma's body as a data storage device?"

"Worse. They're using it as a homing beacon." He grabbed her hand. "If that fragment activates, it will send a signal to whoever planted it. They'll know exactly where we are."

The lights flickered again. This time, they stayed off.

In the darkness, Liang Yue heard something that made her blood run cold.

*Yue'er.*

Grandma's voice. Coming from the drawer.

"Don't open it," Professor Li warned.

But Liang Yue was already reaching for the handle.

The drawer slid open again. Grandma's eyes were open now—but they weren't Grandma's eyes. They were pure black, like pools of oil reflecting nothing. The mouth opened, and a voice that was not quite human spoke.

*The Zhang family does not want the furnace. They want what the furnace can create. The Pill of Eternal Return.*

"What is that?" Liang Yue forced herself to ask.

*A pill that reverses death itself. A pill that brings back the dead.*

"Impossible. There's no such thing."

*There wasn't. Until your grandmother discovered the formula. She hid it from them. She died rather than reveal it.*

The black eyes fixed on Liang Yue, and she felt something cold slide into her mind, searching.

*But you know where it is, don't you? The furnace knows. And now, so do I.*

The drawer slammed shut again. The lights flickered back on.

Professor Li was already pulling her toward the exit. "Run. Run now."

They burst through the morgue doors just as alarms began to blare. Red lights flashed, and somewhere in the distance, Liang Yue heard the sound of running feet.

"They're here," Professor Li gasped. "The Zhang family's security team. They must have had someone monitoring the morgue."

"How many?"

"Enough."

They sprinted through the storage room, back toward the maintenance tunnel. But as they reached the entrance, Liang Yue saw a figure blocking their path.

A woman in a sharp business suit. Late thirties. Perfect makeup. Eyes that held no warmth.

"Miss Liang," the woman said, her voice smooth as silk. "I've been looking for you. My name is Zhang Mei. I believe we have some business to discuss."

"The Zhang family killed my grandmother."

"Your grandmother was a liability. She had information that could have destabilized decades of research. But you—" Zhang Mei smiled. "You're an asset. The last Internal Alchemist. The key to the Pill of Eternal Return."

"I'll never help you."

"Oh, I think you will." Zhang Mei reached into her jacket and pulled out a photograph. "Because I know about your mother."

Liang Yue's heart stopped.

The photograph showed a woman in a hospital bed, hooked up to machines. Her face was thin, pale, ravaged by disease. But Liang Yue recognized her instantly.

"Your mother isn't dead, Miss Liang. She's been in a coma for seventeen years, kept alive by the Zhang family's medical technology. She's been waiting for you to discover the truth."

"Liar."

"Check the hospital records. Chengdu People's Hospital, Ward 7, Bed 12. She's been there the whole time. Your grandmother told you she died in childbirth because she was trying to protect you. But the truth is, your mother was the first person to test the prototype of the Pill of Eternal Return. It failed. It's been failing for seventeen years."

Liang Yue's hands were shaking. The furnace was roaring, but she couldn't think, couldn't breathe.

"Help us complete the formula," Zhang Mei said, "and we'll let your mother go. Refuse, and we'll pull the plug."

Professor Li grabbed Liang Yue's arm. "Don't listen to her. She's manipulating you."

"Am I?" Zhang Mei held up her phone, showing a live video feed. A hospital room. A woman in a bed. Machines beeping.

The woman's eyes opened.

And Liang Yue saw her own reflection staring back.

"Tick-tock," Zhang Mei said.

title: The Dead Speak in Shadow
content:
Liang Yue stared at the stained slide, her medical training forcing her brain to process what her heart rejected. The dark patches clustering around Grandma's hepatic cells weren't natural. They looked like crystals. Like something had grown inside her, slowly, patiently, until—

"Mercury sulfide," Professor Li said, pointing at the largest deposit. "Microscopic particles, embedded in every major organ. Not enough to kill quickly. Enough to kill over six months, if administered in small doses."

"Heavy metal poisoning." Liang Yue's voice came out flat. Clinical. The only way she could speak without screaming. "But mercury sulfide isn't—"

"It's not a standard toxin, no. But it's a standard byproduct." He pulled out another document. "The Zhang family's artificial Qi research produces this exact compound as waste. I found traces in their lab's water runoff last year. When I tried to publish, the journal retracted my paper. The editor died of a heart attack three weeks later."

The furnace pulsed. Hot. Angry. Liang Yue felt its fire spreading through her meridians, demanding release. She forced it down.

"Show me the body."

Professor Li's face went pale. "Liang Yue, you don't understand. The damage—"

"I'm a medical student. I've seen cadavers."

"Not like this. The mercury sulfide preserved her. She looks..." He swallowed. "She looks like she's still alive. But the internal structure is compromised. If you touch her, she might—"

"Show me."

He led her through the empty university morgue, his footsteps echoing on cold tiles. The fluorescent lights hummed overhead, casting everything in sterile white. At the end of the hall, a single door stood closed, marked with a biohazard symbol.

"She's in there." Professor Li's hand trembled on the handle. "I need you to understand something before we go in. The Zhang family knows I have her. They've been watching me for months. The only reason I'm still alive is that I have evidence—enough to expose them if I die suspiciously. But that protection only extends to me."

"You're saying they'll come for me."

"They're already coming. I received this yesterday." He pulled out his phone, showing her a text message from an unknown number. *The old woman's secrets belong to us. Return what was stolen, or the girl suffers.*

Liang Yue's blood ran cold. "They know about me."

"They know about the furnace. They don't know you've awakened it yet. If they did..." He didn't finish the sentence. He didn't need to.

She thought of Grandma's final words. *Don't let them twist the Way.*

"Open the door."

The morgue was cold. Colder than it should have been. The air hung heavy with formaldehyde and something else—something metallic, like old coins and wet stone. A single body lay on the steel table, covered by a white sheet.

Professor Li approached slowly. "I should warn you. The preservation effect means her face is intact, but her body—"

"Just show me."

He pulled back the sheet.

Grandma looked exactly as she had in life. Same weathered face, same silver-streaked hair, same slight smile at the corners of her lips. She could have been sleeping. But Liang Yue's medical eye caught the details: the faint blue tint around her fingernails, the slight swelling at her joints, the way her chest didn't rise.

And something else. Something that made the furnace scream.

There were symbols carved into Grandma's skin. Fine, white lines, almost invisible against her aged complexion. Characters from an ancient script that Liang Yue had never seen, but that her soul recognized.

"What are those?" Her voice cracked.

Professor Li's face went ashen. "I didn't... they weren't there yesterday."

"You've been checking her daily?"

"Every morning. I've been documenting the decomposition process—or lack thereof. Those marks weren't there twelve hours ago."

The symbols were pulsing. Faintly, like a heartbeat, they glowed with a sickly green light. Liang Yue reached out, her fingers hovering above Grandma's arm.

"Don't touch them." Professor Li grabbed her wrist. "I don't know what they are, but—"

"They're alchemical seals." The words came from somewhere deep inside her, from knowledge that wasn't her own. "Binding seals. Someone's trapped her soul."

"What?"

"Grandma was an Internal Alchemist. When she died, her spirit should have dissolved back into the Dao, become part of the world's energy again." Liang Yue's hand shook. "But these seals are keeping her here. They're using her residual Qi as a beacon."

"A beacon for what?"

She didn't answer. She already knew. The furnace was showing her—images flashing behind her eyes like memories that weren't hers. A dark room. Hooded figures. A ritual circle drawn in blood and mercury. Grandma's body at the center, the symbols being carved into her flesh while she still breathed.

They hadn't just killed her. They'd used her death as a key.

"They're trying to open a gate." Liang Yue's voice was barely a whisper. "The Zhang family. They're not just making artificial Qi. They're trying to force a connection to the spirit realm. Grandma's soul is the anchor."

Professor Li stumbled backward. "That's impossible. Alchemy doesn't work that way. The Way can't be forced—"

"They don't believe in the Way. They believe in results." She turned to face him, her eyes burning with the furnace's fire. "And they've been using my grandmother's trapped soul to refine their poison. Every dose of artificial Qi they produce is filtered through her spirit. It's why their test subjects don't die immediately—her residual cultivation is keeping them alive. But it's also why they're becoming something else."

"Becoming what?"

She looked at Grandma's body. At the symbols that pulsed like a dying heartbeat. At the faint smile that suddenly seemed less like peace and more like warning.

"Slaves. The artificial Qi binds the user's spirit to the source. If the Zhang family controls the source, they control everyone who takes their pills." She grabbed the edge of the steel table, her knuckles white. "How many people have they dosed?"

"Dozens. Maybe hundreds. They've been testing on homeless populations, migrant workers, anyone who wouldn't be missed." His voice cracked. "I tried to stop them. I tried to expose them. But every time I got close, someone died."

"Who's leading the research?"

"Zhang Wei. The family's eldest son. He's brilliant—graduated top of his class at Tsinghua, did his doctorate at MIT. He came back to Chengdu three years ago and took over the family's pharmaceutical division." Professor Li's hands were shaking. "He's the one who killed your grandmother. I'm sure of it."

The furnace roared. Fire flooded Liang Yue's meridians, hot enough to burn, hot enough to destroy. She felt the power building, demanding release, demanding vengeance.

But Grandma's voice echoed in her memory. *The Way is not a weapon, Yue'er. It is a path. Those who use it for violence lose themselves.*

She forced the fire down. Locked it in her dantian. Not yet.

"I need to break the seals."

"Liang Yue, if you do that, her soul will dissipate. She'll be gone forever."

"She's already gone." Tears streamed down her face, but her voice was steel. "They're just using her corpse. I won't let them."

"How? You're still learning. These seals are ancient, powerful—"

"They're also wrong." She placed her hands on Grandma's chest, feeling the cold flesh beneath her palms. "They were carved by someone who doesn't understand the Way. They used force instead of flow. I can feel the imbalance."

She closed her eyes. The furnace pulsed in rhythm with her heartbeat. She reached inside herself, found the thread of Qi that connected her to the universe, and pulled.

Fire erupted from her hands.

Not destructive fire—cleansing fire. The flames spread across Grandma's body, tracing the symbols, burning them away one by one. The green light flickered, screamed, fought back. But Liang Yue was the last Internal Alchemist, and the Way flowed through her veins.

The seals shattered.

Grandma's body convulsed. Her eyes snapped open—empty, white, seeing nothing. Her mouth opened, and a voice emerged that wasn't hers, wasn't human.

*Yue'er.*

"Grandma?"

*Listen. I don't have much time.* The voice was fading, dissolving like smoke in wind. *Zhang Wei isn't the real threat. He's a pawn. The one pulling the strings is older. Much older. He's been waiting for the last Internal Alchemist to awaken.*

"Who? Who is it?"

*He's in the mountains. The sacred mountains. He's been trapped there for a thousand years, but the Zhang family is digging him out. They think they're controlling him. They're wrong. When he rises—*

The voice cut off. Grandma's eyes closed. Her body began to dissolve, turning to golden light that scattered upward, toward the ceiling, toward the sky.

*Find the truth in the bamboo. Find the monk who forgot his name. Find—*

She was gone.

Liang Yue collapsed, her hands still outstretched, her heart shattered and reforged in the same instant. The furnace pulsed once, twice, then settled into a steady warmth.

Professor Li rushed to her side. "Are you okay? What happened? What did she say?"

"She said..." Liang Yue struggled to her feet, her legs shaking. "She said Zhang Wei isn't the one we need to fear."

"Then who?"

The morgue door burst open.

Zhang Wei stood in the doorway, flanked by six men in black suits. He was handsome, polished, with the kind of smile that belonged on magazine covers—but his eyes were dead. Flat. Like a snake's.

"Professor Li." His voice was silk over steel. "I see you've been keeping secrets from me."

"Zhang Wei." Professor Li stepped in front of Liang Yue. "She has nothing to do with this."

"On the contrary." Zhang Wei's smile widened. "She has everything to do with this. She's the last Internal Alchemist. She's the key to everything I've been working toward."

He looked at Liang Yue, and his eyes gleamed with something that might have been hunger.

"You've awakened the furnace. I can feel it. The old woman's soul is gone, but you're even more valuable alive." He stepped forward. "Come with me quietly, and I'll let the professor live. Refuse, and I'll kill him, kill your roommates, kill everyone you've ever met. Then I'll take you anyway."

The furnace blazed. Liang Yue's hands ignited with golden fire.

"I'd like to see you try."

Zhang Wei laughed. It was a horrible sound, empty and cold.

"Oh, I'm not going to fight you. I'm going to give you a choice." He pulled out his phone, showing her a live video feed. Her dorm room. Lin Mei and Xiao Fang, tied to chairs, with men in black suits standing over them.

"Your friends have been taking my pills for the past week. The ones you thought were just energy supplements?" He tilted his head. "They're loyal to me now. All it takes is one command, and they'll jump off the roof."

Liang Yue's blood turned to ice.

"Come with me, or they die. And then I'll find more people you love." Zhang Wei's smile never wavered. "You see, Miss Liang, I've been studying

title: The Poison in the Roots
content:
The morgue lights buzzed overhead, fluorescent and sterile, stripping the color from everything they touched. Liang Yue stood over her grandmother's preserved body, the brass furnace thrumming hot against her chest like a second heartbeat.

Grandma looked peaceful. Wrongly peaceful. The kind of peace that came from careful mortician's work, not from a soul at rest.

"She doesn't look poisoned," Liang Yue said. Her voice echoed off the stainless steel walls.

"She wouldn't." Professor Li stood at the door, keeping watch. "The toxin they used—it's called Soul Eater. A compound derived from Nightshade Qi. It breaks down completely within seventy-two hours of death, leaving no trace in standard toxicology screens."

"Then how did you find it?"

"I didn't look for the toxin." He stepped closer, pointing to the liver cross-section. "I looked for the scars. Soul Eater doesn't just kill—it eats through the meridians, the energy channels. When it breaks down, it leaves micro-scarring on the organs. Like burn marks from the inside."

Liang Yue leaned in. There. Fine white lines, branching like lightning across the organ's surface. She'd seen similar patterns before—in Grandma's alchemy notes, in diagrams of spiritual damage from failed cultivation.

*When Qi burns the body from within, the scars look like frozen lightning.*

"The Zhang family gave her this," Liang Yue whispered. It wasn't a question.

"Someone did. Whether it was Zhang personally or one of his enforcers—" Professor Li shrugged. "The result is the same."

"Why didn't she fight back?" Liang Yue's hands clenched. "She was the strongest alchemist in a century. She could have—"

"She could have destroyed them." Professor Li's voice was gentle. "But she chose not to. Because fighting back would have exposed the Way to the world. Would have started a war between alchemists and modern pharmaceuticals. She chose to die quietly so that you could live to carry on the tradition."

The furnace burned hotter. Liang Yue felt tears streaming down her face, but her voice stayed steady.

"That's not a choice. That's murder dressed up as sacrifice."

"Sometimes they're the same thing."

A knock at the morgue door. Three sharp raps, then two.

Professor Li's face went pale. "That's my emergency signal. Someone's coming."

"Who?"

"I don't know. But they know we're here." He grabbed the folder, shoved it into Liang Yue's hands. "Take this. Go out the back—through the cadaver storage, there's a service exit that leads to the parking garage."

"What about you?"

"I'll delay them. I'm just a professor doing late-night research. You're breaking and entering."

"I have the furnace. I can—"

"You can't fight them yet." He gripped her shoulders, his eyes fierce. "You're strong, Liang Yue. But you're not ready. The Zhang family has decades of resources, connections, and artificial Qi soldiers. You have a furnace and three months of practice."

"I have Grandma's notes."

"You have the beginning of her notes. The full inheritance—" He shook his head. "That takes years to unlock. Decades. You have to survive long enough to learn."

Another knock. Louder this time. A voice through the door: "Professor Li? Campus security. We received a report of unauthorized access to the morgue."

"Go," he hissed.

Liang Yue ran.

The cadaver storage room was cold enough to see her breath. Body bags lined the walls, each tagged with a name and date. She moved between them, her footsteps echoing on the tile floor. The service exit was at the far end, a heavy steel door with a push-bar.

She was ten feet away when the lights went out.

Complete darkness. The kind that pressed against your eyes, disorienting and absolute. Liang Yue froze, her heart hammering.

*The furnace,* Grandma's voice whispered in her memory. *When darkness falls, let the furnace light your way.*

She focused. Pulled Qi from her dantian, channeled it through her meridians, into the furnace. The brass vessel pulsed once, twice—then emitted a soft golden glow, just enough to see by.

The service exit was still there. But so was something else.

A figure stood between her and the door. Tall, thin, dressed in black. The face was hidden in shadow, but the eyes—the eyes glowed faintly red, like embers in a dying fire.

"You're the new one," the figure said. Voice like gravel and broken glass. "The granddaughter."

"Who are you?"

"Someone who's been waiting a long time for the furnace to pass to new hands." The figure stepped forward, and the golden light from Liang Yue's furnace caught their face.

It was a woman. Maybe forty, maybe sixty—hard to tell with the burn scars that covered half her features. The skin was twisted and melted, pulling one eye into a permanent squint. But the other eye—the red eye—was fixed on the furnace with hunger.

"I was your grandmother's first student," the woman said. "Before she decided I wasn't worthy of the Way."

"You're a Zhang."

"I'm no one's tool. I'm my own woman, with my own path." She smiled, and the scars pulled grotesquely. "But I do work with them. They pay well, and they don't ask questions about my methods."

"What do you want?"

"The furnace. The notes. Everything your grandmother denied me." She reached into her coat, pulled out a syringe filled with black liquid. "Don't make this painful. Give me the furnace, and I'll let you walk out of here."

"You'll kill me anyway."

"Probably. But it'll be quick." She shrugged. "Or I can inject you with Soul Eater concentrate and watch you dissolve from the inside. Your choice."

Liang Yue's mind raced. She couldn't fight—the woman was clearly an experienced cultivator, probably decades of training. She couldn't run—the door was behind the woman. She couldn't negotiate—the woman wanted the furnace, nothing else.

But she could stall.

"Grandma never mentioned a first student."

"Of course she didn't. I was her shame. Her failure." The woman's voice cracked. "I gave her everything. Ten years of my life. And she cast me aside because I wanted to use the Way for power, not enlightenment."

"Power corrupts the Way."

"That's what she said." The woman laughed, bitter and sharp. "And then she died, and the Way passed to a pharmacy student who can barely refine a basic Healing Pill. Tell me—is that enlightenment? Or is it waste?"

"Enlightenment isn't about power."

"No. It's about who gets to decide." The woman raised the syringe. "I've been refining artificial Qi for fifteen years. I've perfected a compound that can simulate the effects of the furnace—without the moralizing, without the restrictions. With the furnace, I can mass-produce it. Heal the sick. Transform the world."

"Or destroy it."

"Every transformation destroys something." She stepped closer. "Enough talking. Hand it over."

Liang Yue's hand went to her chest. The furnace was hot now, almost painful. It was responding to her fear, her anger, her desperation.

*The furnace chooses its master,* Grandma had said. *But a master must also choose their path.*

She made her choice.

"Come and take it."

The woman lunged.

Liang Yue didn't try to fight. She dropped to the ground, rolled sideways, and shoved her hand into the nearest body bag. Cold flesh. Tag. She grabbed the tag, ripped it off, and threw it at the woman's face.

The woman batted it away, but the distraction was enough. Liang Yue scrambled to her feet, grabbed a metal tray from the counter, and swung it like a shield.

The syringe pierced the tray, the needle snapping. Black liquid splattered across the floor, hissing as it ate through the tile.

"You little—"

The door burst open. Light flooded the room.

"Campus security! Freeze!"

The woman snarled, her red eye blazing. She looked at Liang Yue, at the broken syringe, at the security guards rushing in.

"This isn't over, granddaughter." She backed toward the wall, pulled something from her pocket—a small device that crackled with electricity. "I'll find you. And when I do, I'll take everything you love."

She pressed the device. A flash of light. When Liang Yue's vision cleared, the woman was gone.

The security guards surrounded her, flashlights blinding. "Hands up! Don't move!"

Liang Yue raised her hands. The furnace pulsed once, then went cold and silent.

She was caught.

The campus security office smelled like stale coffee and cheap air freshener. Liang Yue sat in a plastic chair, her hands cuffed in front of her, while two officers reviewed the footage from the morgue.

"There's nothing on the cameras," one said, frowning. "Just you, the professor, and then static."

"That's because she disabled them."

"Who?"

"I don't know her name. A woman with burn scars. She wanted to steal—" Liang Yue stopped herself. She couldn't mention the furnace. "She wanted to steal my grandmother's research."

"Your grandmother's body is in that morgue for official research purposes. You're not authorized to access it."

"I know. I'm sorry."

The officer sighed. "Professor Li vouched for you. Said you were helping him with a project. We're not pressing charges, but you're banned from the medical campus for six months."

"Understood."

They uncuffed her. She rubbed her wrists, stood, and walked out into the cold night air.

Professor Li was waiting by his car. His face was drawn, worried.

"She got away," he said.

"I know."

"She'll try again. She knows you're the heir now."

"I know."

"There's something else." He handed her a phone—not hers, a burner. "This was in my office when I got back. No sender, no return address."

Liang Yue turned it on. A single message glowed on the screen:

*The Zhang family isn't your enemy. They're your family. Come home, cousin.*

She stared at the words. The furnace flared hot, then cold, then hot again.

"What does it mean?" Professor Li asked.

"I don't know." But her blood was ice. Cousin? The Zhang family wasn't her family. Her family was Grandma. Her family was the Liangs.

Wasn't it?

The phone buzzed again. A second message:

*Your mother didn't die in a car accident. She left. She's alive. And she's waiting for you.*

Liang Yue's world cracked.

Grandma had always said her mother died when she was three. A car accident on the mountain roads. No body recovered—the car had gone over a cliff into the river.

But what if that was a lie too?

What if everything Grandma had told her was a lie?

She looked at the phone. Her thumb hovered over the reply button.

*Come home, cousin.*

She typed three words:

*Where is home?*

The response came instantly:

*The Zhang Corporation Tower. 88th floor. Ask for Madam Zhang. She's been waiting twenty years to meet you.*

Liang Yue looked up at the sky. The stars were hidden by Chengdu's perpetual smog, but somewhere beyond it, the moon was full.

She thought of Grandma's final words. *Don't let them take it. Don't let them twist the Way.*

But what if the Way had already been twisted?

What if she was the one who had been twisted all along

title: The Morgue's Hidden Truth
content:
The chemical marker on Grandma's liver looked like a constellation of dying stars—pinpricks of blue-black against the stained tissue, clustered around the bile ducts like poison seeking escape. Liang Yue's medical training kicked in, her finger tracing the pattern automatically.

"That's not natural," she breathed.

"No." Professor Li turned to another page. "The toxicology report shows a compound they couldn't identify. Officially, it's listed as 'unusual metabolic byproduct consistent with advanced age.' But I had a friend at the CDC run it through their database."

"And?"

"It matches something called Compound 7-Alpha. A synthetic derivative of arsenic combined with a rare earth element—neodymium, if you can believe it. Developed in the 1980s by a Chinese pharmaceutical company that went bankrupt under mysterious circumstances."

Liang Yue's stomach turned. "The Zhang family."

"Indirectly. The company was a shell corporation, but the patent holder was a man named Zhang Tianming. Your grandmother's former apprentice."

The name hit like a punch to the chest. "She never mentioned an apprentice."

"Because he betrayed her. Thirty years ago, he tried to steal the furnace. She caught him, expelled him from the tradition. But he took something with him—her research notes on artificial Qi synthesis."

The furnace vibrated in Liang Yue's dantian, a warning pulse that made her gasp. "She never told me any of this."

"She was protecting you." Professor Li's voice was gentle but firm. "The Internal Alchemy tradition has rules. The furnace chooses the successor, but the knowledge is passed down through direct teaching. If Zhang Tianming learned even a fraction of the theory—"

"He could reverse-engineer the pills."

"Worse. He could create counterfeits that look real but contain slow-acting poisons. Control the market, eliminate competition, build a monopoly on supernatural healing."

Liang Yue thought of the patients she'd been treating in secret—the leukemia boy whose bone marrow had regenerated, the old woman whose blindness had reversed, the young man whose Qi deviation had nearly killed him. Each one had required specific pills, specific timings, specific attention to their unique energy patterns.

If someone started selling cheap imitations, people would die.

"I need to see the body," she said again. "The photos aren't enough."

Professor Li hesitated. "The morgue is monitored. Chief Medical Examiner Wang is a Zhang family operative. If he sees you—"

"Then we don't let him see me."

She pulled out her phone, scrolling through her contacts. Two weeks of operating in the shadows of Chengdu's supernatural underground had taught her things—like how to find people who owed favors, and how to collect them.

"Xiao Mei," she said when the call connected. "I need your help."

The girl on the other end was a third-year biology student with a talent for hacking that bordered on supernatural. She'd been one of Liang Yue's first patients—a Qi deviation that had nearly destroyed her cultivation base, fixed by a single Meridian Alignment Pill.

"For you, Sister Yue? Anything."

"Can you loop the morgue security cameras for thirty minutes? Starting at midnight?"

A pause. "The university morgue? That's run by—"

"I know who runs it. Can you do it or not?"

The silence stretched. Liang Yue could hear Xiao Mei's fingers flying across a keyboard.

"There's a backdoor in the system. A forgotten admin account from when the software was installed. Give me two hours."

"Thank you."

"Be careful, Sister Yue. Something's wrong with that morgue. I've seen strange data logs—body movements recorded during hours when no staff should be there."

The call ended. Liang Yue turned back to Professor Li, who was watching her with a mixture of concern and admiration.

"You've built quite a network in two weeks."

"Desperate people find each other." She tucked the photos into her bag. "I'll go alone. If something happens—"

"I know. Destroy the furnace or hide it where they can't find it."

"No. If something happens, you go to the Guo family. Tell them everything. They're the only ones who might stand against the Zhangs."

The Guo family was one of Chengdu's oldest supernatural lineages—traditionalists who believed in the old ways, who had watched the Zhangs' rise with growing alarm. They'd offered Liang Yue protection twice already. She'd refused both times, not wanting to owe favors to another power player.

But if she was about to walk into a trap, she needed backup.

---

Midnight in Chengdu. The city that never slept was quieter than usual, a cold front rolling in from the mountains bringing fog and drizzle. Liang Yue moved through the shadows of the medical campus, her footsteps silent on the wet pavement.

The morgue was a squat concrete building behind the main hospital, its windows dark, its single entrance locked with a keycard system she'd already disabled. She slipped through the door and into a hallway that smelled of formaldehyde and bleach.

The lights were motion-activated, flickering on as she passed, then dying behind her. The furnace pulsed with each step, a heartbeat in her dantian that seemed to synchronize with something deeper in the building.

*Grandma,* she thought. *Guide me.*

She found the cold storage room at the end of the hall. The door was unlocked—unusual for a facility that handled potentially contagious remains. She pushed it open and stepped inside.

Rows of refrigerated drawers lined the walls, each one labeled with a name and date. She found Grandma's drawer near the back, the name "CHEN, LI-HUA" written in neat block letters.

Her hands trembled as she pulled the drawer open.

Grandma looked peaceful. Almost alive. The embalming had preserved her features, smoothed the wrinkles from her face, made her look like she was sleeping. Liang Yue had to remind herself that this wasn't her grandmother anymore—just a shell, a vessel that had held a great soul.

But the body told its own story.

She pulled out the photos and began comparing. The toxicology report had identified Compound 7-Alpha in the liver, but the autopsy photos showed something else—a faint discoloration in the right lung, a shadow that didn't match the official narrative of cardiac arrest.

She leaned closer, her breath fogging the cold metal surface.

The discoloration was geometric.

A pattern of hexagons, arranged in a honeycomb formation, invisible to the naked eye but clear under the chemical staining. She'd seen this pattern before—in the internal structure of certain pills, the way Qi flowed through the Meridian Alignment formula.

Grandma's lung had been used as a cultivation vessel.

"Son of a bitch," Liang Yue whispered.

"They were growing something inside her."

The voice came from behind her. She spun, her hand reaching for the defensive pill she always carried.

A figure stood in the doorway—tall, thin, wearing a white lab coat over a black suit. His face was a mask of professional courtesy, but his eyes were cold and calculating.

Chief Medical Examiner Wang.

"Miss Liang," he said, his voice smooth as oil. "I was wondering when you'd come."

"You knew I'd be here."

"The cameras went offline. That was too convenient to be coincidence." He stepped into the room, his hands clasped behind his back. "I have to say, I'm impressed. Your grandmother taught you well."

"Stay away from me."

"I'm not here to hurt you. I'm here to offer you a choice." He pulled out a small vial, filled with a golden liquid that seemed to glow from within. "This is what they were trying to create. Artificial Qi, synthesized and purified. Perfect for cultivation, without the years of training."

"That's not Qi. That's poison."

"Only if you don't know how to use it." He set the vial on a nearby counter. "The Zhang family wants to hire you. Full partnership, access to their labs, resources beyond your imagination. You could heal thousands with what they've developed."

"I heal people now. Without selling my soul."

"But for how long?" Wang's smile was razor-thin. "You're the last Internal Alchemist. When you die, the tradition dies with you. Unless you train successors. Unless you share the knowledge."

"The furnace chooses the successor. I can't force it."

"No. But you can give us the formula. Let us mass-produce the pills, spread the healing to everyone who needs it. Think of the good you could do."

Liang Yue's hand closed around the defensive pill. "I think of what happened to my grandmother. She trusted someone once. It killed her."

"Your grandmother was a fool who clung to outdated traditions. The world is changing, Miss Liang. Either you change with it, or you're crushed by it."

The furnace blazed in her dantian, screaming a warning. She felt it a second too late—the subtle shift in the air, the faint smell of something burning.

The body in the drawer was moving.

Grandma's eyes snapped open.

They were wrong. Wrong color—pupil-less gold, like coins from some forgotten dynasty. The mouth opened, and a voice emerged that wasn't Grandma's voice—ancient, cold, layered with harmonics that made the air vibrate.

"Little alchemist," the voice said. "We've been waiting."

Grandma's hand shot out, cold fingers wrapping around Liang Yue's wrist with impossible strength. The furnace screamed in response, and Liang Yue felt her Qi draining, pulled into the body, into the golden eyes, into whatever had taken residence in her grandmother's corpse.

"The body is a furnace," the voice continued. "And this one has been prepared for centuries. You will give us the key, or you will join her."

Wang was laughing now, his professional mask gone, replaced by naked greed. "You see, Miss Liang? We've already reverse-engineered the body cultivation. Your grandmother's corpse is now a vessel for an ancient spirit—a Furnace King, from the time when alchemists could forge souls instead of pills."

"You monster," Liang Yue gasped, struggling against the grip.

"No. I'm a scientist. And science doesn't have ethics. It only has results."

The golden eyes grew brighter. The grip tightened. Liang Yue felt her consciousness starting to slip, the boundaries between herself and the spirit blurring.

Then she remembered something Grandma had taught her. A desperation move, never to be used unless there was no other choice.

*When the body is stolen, when the spirit is trapped, there is only one escape. The Pill of Self-Annihilation.*

She reached into her pocket, fingers closing around a small black pill she'd made in secret, never thinking she'd need it.

"I'll die first," she whispered.

Wang's eyes widened. "Don't be stupid. You can't—"

But Liang Yue had already brought the pill to her lips.

The door exploded inward.

A figure moved through the smoke and debris—a woman in traditional Daoist robes, her hair white as snow, her eyes burning with a fire that matched the furnace in Liang Yue's dantian.

"Get away from her," the woman said, her voice carrying the weight of centuries.

Grandma's body released Liang Yue, turning to face the newcomer. The golden eyes flickered with recognition.

"You," the corpse-voice hissed. "We thought you were dead."

"I was." The woman stepped forward, a jade sword appearing in her hand. "But death is just another transformation."

And then everything went white.

title: The Poison in the Blood
content:
The chemical marker glowed like tiny stars against the dark tissue of Grandma's liver—except stars didn't cluster in geometric patterns. These did. Triangles within triangles, precise as a blueprint.

"This is a formation," Liang Yue whispered. "Embedded in her cells."

Professor Li's face tightened. "You can see it?"

"Clear as day." She traced the pattern with her finger, not touching the photo. "This isn't a poison. It's a seal. Someone locked her Qi circulation from the inside."

"That would explain the autopsy discrepancy." He pulled out another sheet. "The medical examiner noted unusual cellular arrangement but couldn't identify a cause. I assumed it was a rare toxin, but if you're saying it's alchemical..."

"It's alchemical." Liang Yue's voice dropped. "But it's wrong. Internal Alchemy builds from within, refines what's already there. This—" she tapped the image, "—this is external force forcing a change. Violent. Destructive."

"Artificial Qi."

The words hung between them like a death sentence.

Professor Li closed the folder. "The Zhang family has been working with a chemist named Dr. Yuki Tanaka. She defected from a Japanese pharmaceutical conglomerate two years ago, brought her research with her. Word is she cracked the problem of synthetic cultivation compounds."

"Synthetic cultivation." Liang Yue laughed, bitter and sharp. "That's like saying synthetic digestion. It doesn't work that way."

"It doesn't have to work. It just has to seem like it works." He pulled up a news article on his phone, turned the screen toward her. "Look."

The headline read: ZHANG FAMILY BIOTECH ANNOUNCES MIRACLE REGENERATIVE TREATMENT. The photo showed a smiling woman in a hospital bed, her skin smooth and young despite the grey in her hair.

"This is a sixty-eight-year-old woman," Professor Li said. "She was diagnosed with terminal liver cancer six months ago. After three treatments with Zhang Biotech's new compound, her tumors shrank by forty percent."

"That's not possible unless—" Liang Yue stopped.

"Unless what?"

Unless the compound was feeding on the tumors. Unless it was converting the woman's own life force into something the Zhang family could harvest.

She didn't say it aloud. She couldn't, not yet, not without proof.

"When's the next treatment scheduled?"

Professor Li's eyes narrowed. "Three days from now. At their private clinic in the High-Tech Zone."

"Can you get me in?"

"Absolutely not. If they find out who you are—"

"Then they can't find out." She pulled out her phone, already scrolling through contacts. "I know someone who owes me. Chen Wei, from the pharmacy program. His mother works at Zhang Biotech as a cleaner."

"Liang Yue—"

"I'm not asking permission." Her voice was steel wrapped in silk. "I'm telling you what's going to happen."

The furnace pulsed in her dantian, hotter than before. It had been doing that more frequently since Grandma's death—reacting to threats, to lies, to the presence of corrupted Qi. She was learning to read its language.

This one meant danger.

This one meant *kill*.

---

Chen Wei's apartment smelled like instant noodles and regret. He opened the door with red-rimmed eyes and a textbook still open on his desk.

"Liang Yue? It's two in the morning."

"I know. I need a favor."

He blinked at her, then at the dark street behind her, then back at her face. "Is this about your grandmother? I heard—"

"It's about the Zhang family. Your mother works for them."

His face closed like a door slamming shut. "I can't help you."

"You haven't heard what I'm asking."

"I don't need to." He stepped back, motioning for her to come inside. "My mom's been acting strange for months. Coming home late, losing weight, forgetting things. I thought it was stress. Then last week I found this in her bag."

He pulled out a small vial from his desk drawer. The liquid inside was pale gold, almost luminous.

"Where did she get this?"

"She said it was a vitamin supplement. From the company clinic." Chen Wei's hands trembled as he held it out. "But vitamins don't glow."

Liang Yue took the vial, uncapped it, held it close to her nose. The smell was familiar—metallic, sweet, with an undertone of rotting flowers. She'd encountered it once before, in Grandma's medicine cabinet, hidden behind a bottle of traditional herbal pills.

"Don't let her take any more," she said, her voice barely controlled. "This isn't a vitamin. It's a binding agent."

"A what?"

"A primer. It doesn't do anything by itself, but when combined with another compound—" She stopped. The pieces were falling into place with terrible clarity. "The Zhang family isn't just making artificial Qi. They're making *consumers*."

"What are you talking about?"

"They need people to test their compounds on. But they can't use just anyone—the compounds have to bond with the subject's natural Qi field. So they're seeding the population with binding agents, getting people ready." She held up the vial. "This is the seed. The treatments are the harvest."

Chen Wei's face went pale. "My mom—"

"Is a test subject. They're all test subjects."

The furnace roared in her chest. She pressed a hand to her abdomen, forcing it to calm. Not yet. Not here.

"I need to see your mother," she said. "I need to know what stage she's at."

"She won't tell me anything. She thinks I'm being paranoid."

"Then I'll talk to her as a friend. Just get me into the clinic."

Chen Wei was silent for a long moment. Then he nodded, slow and reluctant. "There's a shift change at six AM. My mom works the overnight cleaning crew. I can get you a uniform."

"Good."

"You're going to do something dangerous, aren't you?"

Liang Yue looked at the vial, still glowing faintly in her hand. She thought of Grandma's face in those final days—the confusion, the weakness, the way her Qi had been systematically unraveled from within.

"I'm going to finish what my grandmother started."

---

The Zhang Biotech facility was a glass-and-steel monument to corporate ambition. Fourteen stories of gleaming windows and security cameras, surrounded by a wall topped with razor wire. The parking lot was full of expensive cars.

Liang Yue pulled the cleaner's uniform tighter around herself, feeling the weight of the furnace in her dantian. She'd spent the last three hours preparing—grinding herbs, activating pills, weaving protective formations into her skin with medicinal ink. The marks were invisible to normal eyes, but she could feel them pulsing in time with her heartbeat.

Chen Wei's mother, Mrs. Chen, was a small woman with tired eyes and hands rough from years of work. She barely glanced at Liang Yue as they passed through the employee entrance.

"Stay close," she murmured. "The night supervisor checks the fourth floor at three. We need to be done by then."

"What are we cleaning on the fourth floor?"

"Laboratory wing. They've been doing experiments late into the night." Mrs. Chen's voice was flat, mechanical. "They don't like us seeing what's in the rooms."

Perfect.

They moved through the building like ghosts, cleaning carts rattling behind them. Liang Yue memorized the layout—stairwells, exits, security camera blind spots. The furnace pulsed with each step, guiding her toward something she couldn't yet see.

The fourth floor was different. The air was colder, the lights dimmer, the walls lined with pipes that hummed with contained energy. Liang Yue felt it before she saw it—a wrongness in the Qi field, like a discordant note in a symphony.

"Wait here," she told Mrs. Chen. "If anyone comes, say I'm in the bathroom."

"Be careful."

Liang Yue slipped through a door marked AUTHORIZED PERSONNEL ONLY. The hallway beyond was lined with doors, each with a small window. She peered through the first one.

A lab. Rows of beakers and centrifuges. A whiteboard covered in chemical equations that made no sense.

The second door: an office. Files stacked on a desk. A half-eaten meal.

The third door: she stopped breathing.

The room beyond was filled with cages. Human-sized cages, each containing a person. They sat in silence, their eyes open but unseeing, their skin pale and waxy. Tubes ran from their arms to machines that hummed and clicked.

Liang Yue pressed her hand against the glass. The furnace screamed in her chest.

*They're still alive.*

*They're still alive and they're being drained.*

She forced herself to look. Nine cages. Nine people. All in various stages of Qi depletion—their life forces being slowly extracted, converted, stored in the vats that lined the far wall.

And in the center of the room, mounted on a pedestal, was a furnace.

It looked like hers. Brass body, eight trigrams carved into the surface, a lid shaped like a coiled dragon. But where Grandma's furnace pulsed with warm golden light, this one glowed sickly green.

A perversion. A corruption.

A copy.

Liang Yue's hand was on the door handle before she could stop herself.

"Don't."

The voice came from behind her. She spun.

A woman stood in the hallway. Japanese, mid-forties, wearing a white lab coat. Her eyes were sharp, calculating, and completely without mercy.

"Dr. Yuki Tanaka, I presume."

"You presume correctly." Tanaka smiled, thin and cold. "And you must be the grandmother's successor. I was wondering when you'd show up."

"Let these people go."

"Let them go?" Tanaka laughed. "They're volunteers. Paid handsomely for their participation in medical research."

"Research into what?"

"Into the nature of Qi itself." Tanaka stepped closer. "You Internal Alchemists have hoarded your knowledge for centuries. Guarded your secrets like dragons guarding gold. But science doesn't bow to tradition." She gestured at the room. "I've replicated what took your family eight hundred years to develop. In two years."

"You've replicated nothing. That furnace is a corpse."

"It works."

"It *steals*." Liang Yue's voice rose. "It doesn't refine. It drains. You're killing these people."

"Collateral damage." Tanaka shrugged. "Progress requires sacrifice. Your grandmother understood that."

"My grandmother died fighting people like you."

"No." Tanaka's smile widened. "Your grandmother died because I was *better*."

The world went red.

Liang Yue moved before she thought, her hand reaching for the pill pouch at her belt. But Tanaka was faster—she pressed a button on her watch, and the floor beneath Liang Yue's feet opened.

She fell.

Three stories of darkness, her body twisting, her Qi flaring to cushion the impact. She hit concrete hard, pain shooting through her legs. The furnace pulsed, healing, but slow—too slow.

Above her, Tanaka's voice echoed through the shaft.

"Lock her in the sub-basement. She'll make an excellent test subject."

Liang Yue tried to stand. Her ankle screamed in protest. The darkness pressed in around her, thick and cold, and somewhere in the distance, she heard the sound of bars slamming shut.

The furnace pulsed once, twice, then went silent.

For the first time since Grandma's death, Liang Yue felt truly alone.

title: Chapter 16
content:
CHAPTER 16: The Wasteland Beneath the City

The morgue's fluorescent lights hummed with the sound of trapped insects. Liang Yue stood over her grandmother's preserved body, the brass furnace burning cold fire in her dantian, and tried to reconcile the wrinkled face before her with the woman who had taught her to crush herbs at age six.

Professor Li stood by the door, one hand pressed against his chest like he was checking his own heartbeat. "The toxicology report shows a compound I've never seen before. It bonds with mitochondrial DNA and releases slowly over months. By the time symptoms appear, it's everywhere."

"You said the Zhang family." Liang Yue's voice came out flat. Empty. "But you didn't say who specifically."

"Zhang Wei controls the pharmaceutical division. His sister, Zhang Mei, runs the research wing. Their father, Zhang Tianlong, is the patriarch." Professor Li's jaw tightened. "But the one who ordered your grandmother's death was likely the grandmother herself."

Liang Yue's head snapped up. "The grandmother?"

"Zhang Lin. Matriarch of the Zhang family. She's one hundred and seven years old and still runs the family council with an iron fist." He paused. "She's also dying. Has been for twenty years. The family has been searching for a way to extend her life through artificial means."

"And Grandma had the real thing."

"Your grandmother had the only thing that could save Zhang Lin without killing her. Internal Alchemy doesn't just extend life—it transforms it. The Zhang family has been trying to replicate that for decades." He gestured to the body. "When your grandmother refused to share the furnace's secrets, they decided to take them by force."

Liang Yue's hands clenched into fists. "Then they failed. I have the furnace. I have the recipes. And I will never—"

"Miss Liang." A new voice cut through the morgue's chill. "That's exactly what we're counting on."

Professor Li spun around. Liang Yue's blood turned to ice.

A woman stood in the doorway. She was young—no older than thirty—with sharp features and eyes that held the cold calculation of a predator. Her suit was immaculate, her hair pulled back in a severe bun, and her smile was the most terrifying thing Liang Yue had seen in weeks.

"Zhang Mei," Professor Li breathed.

"Professor Li." Zhang Mei inclined her head. "I always knew you were more than a simple academic. Shame about your retirement fund, by the way. The university board just voted to freeze it pending an investigation into misuse of research materials."

"Get out," Liang Yue said. "This is a restricted area."

"This is my city." Zhang Mei stepped inside, her heels clicking against the tile floor. "And you're standing over the body of a woman my family tried very hard to bury without anyone asking questions." She stopped ten feet away, studying Liang Yue with clinical interest. "You're smaller than I expected. The last Internal Alchemist, and you look like you should be studying for finals."

"Leave her alone," Professor Li said. "She doesn't know—"

"She knows enough." Zhang Mei's smile widened. "She knows her grandmother was murdered. She knows we want the furnace. And she knows that sooner or later, she'll have to choose between giving it up and dying the same way."

Liang Yue's hand went to her dantian. The furnace pulsed, heat building. "You killed my grandmother."

"We removed an obstacle." Zhang Mei shrugged. "Your grandmother was a relic of a dying tradition. She hoarded knowledge that could save millions. Diabetes. Cancer. Aging itself. All of it locked away in recipes she refused to share because of some ancient code about spiritual purity." She shook her head. "We're trying to save the world, Miss Liang. Your grandmother was trying to preserve a museum."

"She was trying to protect the Way."

"The Way is dead." Zhang Mei's voice hardened. "Cultivation died when the last spiritual vein dried up three hundred years ago. What your grandmother practiced was a ghost—a memory of power that no longer exists. But we can recreate it. We can synthesize it. We can give humanity the benefits of alchemy without the centuries of practice."

"And you're using human test subjects."

"Volunteers." Zhang Mei's eyes flickered. "Desperate people. Terminal patients who have nothing to lose. We give them hope. We give them a chance." She paused. "Some of them even survive."

"Some of them."

"The ones who don't die become something else." Zhang Mei reached into her pocket and pulled out a small vial. Inside, a liquid swirled—black and viscous, like oil mixed with blood. "This is our latest breakthrough. We call it Primordial Qi. It's not real Qi, of course. But it can simulate the effects of cultivation for up to six hours."

"Six hours of power that destroys the body's natural energy pathways."

"Six hours of power that lets a dying man walk again. That lets a blind woman see. That lets a broken soldier fight." Zhang Mei held up the vial. "The side effects are temporary. Reversible. We're close to fixing them entirely."

"You're close to mass-producing poison."

"Your grandmother said the same thing." Zhang Mei's smile vanished. "And then she died. Coincidence, the coroner ruled. Heart failure in an elderly woman." She pocketed the vial.
