"With your grandmother's recipes." He nodded. "She had the complete collection of Zhang Xian's original works. They've been searching for them for centuries."

Liang Yue looked down at Grandma's peaceful face. "She destroyed them. Before she died. She burned everything."

"I know. I was there."

"You were there?"

"She called me. Said she needed help. I drove to her house and found her in the garden, feeding pages into a metal barrel. Flames twenty feet high." He smiled sadly. "She looked like a witch from an old story. Her white hair flying in the wind, her eyes fierce."

"Why didn't she just give them to the police?"

"Because the police would have returned them to the Zhang family. The Zhangs have been collecting Internal Alchemy texts for generations. Your grandmother's collection was the last independent source." He touched the furnace on her chest. "And that was the only thing they wanted more than the texts."

"They killed her for a furnace."

"They killed her because she refused to let them pervert the Way."

Liang Yue touched Grandma's cold cheek. "I'll make them pay."

"No." Professor Li grabbed her arm. "That's exactly what they want. They want you to come after them. They want you to use the furnace in anger, to make mistakes."

"What do you suggest I do instead?"

"Learn. Grow stronger. Master the furnace." He met her eyes. "Make the Pill of Three Realms Awakening."

"That's insane. I've only been an alchemist for three weeks."

"Your grandmother believed you could do it." He held up the recipe. "She left this for you, knowing exactly who you are and what you can become."

"But the ingredients—the flower that blooms once a century, the mulberry tree struck by lightning—"

"I can help you find them. I've spent thirty years studying alchemy. I know where these things can be found." He paused. "But there's a price."

"What price?"

"The blood of the alchemist. Drawn under a new moon." He looked at her seriously. "That's not just a metaphor. You'll have to give a piece of yourself to the furnace. It will bond to you permanently. There's no going back."

"What does that mean, exactly?"

"It means you'll never be able to live a normal life again. You'll be tied to the alchemical world forever. The furnace will become a part of you—your thoughts, your emotions, your very soul."

Liang Yue looked down at Grandma's face. She thought of the Zhang family, poisoning an old woman for a brass pot. She thought of artificial Qi, of test subjects, of a world where alchemy was twisted into a weapon.

She thought of the recipe in her hands, written in Grandma's blood and tears.

"I don't want a normal life," she said. "I want justice."

Professor Li's face softened. "Then we'd better start tonight." He checked his watch. "The new moon is in three days."

"Where do we find the ingredients?"

"The mulberry tree is easy. There's one on the university campus that was struck by lightning last year. I've been saving samples." He pulled out a small glass vial from his pocket. "The morning dew is harder. We need a lotus pond at dawn."

"I know one. In the old quarter of Chengdu. Grandma used to take me there."

"Good. The flower is the hardest part."

"What kind of flower is it?"

"The Ghost Orchid. It blooms once every hundred years, and only under a full moon." He paused. "There's one in the Zhang family's private greenhouse."

Liang Yue's heart dropped. "They have it."

"They guard it like a treasure. It's the only surviving specimen in the world." He smiled grimly. "We're going to have to steal it."

"Steal from the Zhang family?"

"Three days from now, during the new moon, they'll be holding a ceremony. A celebration of their artificial Qi breakthrough." He pulled out a folder. "The greenhouse will be lightly guarded. Everyone will be at the party."

"How do you know all this?"

"Because I've been investigating them for ten years." He pulled out a badge. Silver, with a dragon embossed on the front. "I work for the Ministry of State Security. Alchemical Crimes Division."

Liang Yue's jaw dropped. "You're a government agent?"

"Technically, I'm a professor. But I have a second job." He tucked the badge away. "The Ministry has been watching the Zhang family for years. We just haven't had enough evidence to move against them."

"Until now."

"Until now." He held up the toxicology report. "This is enough to get a warrant. But it'll take time to process. Time we don't have."

"The Pill of Three Realms Awakening," Liang Yue said slowly. "If I make it, I can stop them."

"You can access the Heaven realm. See through their illusions. Track their movements." He nodded. "You can become something they've never faced before."

"A real Internal Alchemist."

"A true Internal Alchemist." He smiled. "The last one."

Liang Yue looked at Grandma one last time. She leaned down and kissed her forehead.

"I'll make you proud, Grandma."

She turned to Professor Li. "Let's go steal a flower."

---

The Zhang Pharmaceutical Research Center loomed against the night sky like a fortress.

Eight stories of glass and steel, surrounded by a twelve-foot wall topped with razor wire. Guard towers at each corner. Patrols circling the perimeter.

And in the center, a glass dome that glittered under the moonlight.

"The greenhouse," Professor Li whispered.

They were crouched behind a parked delivery truck, two blocks away. He handed her a pair of night-vision goggles.

"I count twelve guards," he said. "Plus motion sensors, thermal cameras, and pressure plates in the garden."

"How are we supposed to get past all that?"

"We're not." He pulled out a small device from his backpack. "I've been working on this for years. A portable Qi disruptor. It creates a localized field that interferes with electronic surveillance."

title: The Blood on the Floor
content:
The morgue's fluorescent lights hummed like trapped insects, casting everything in that particular shade of green that made death look even more clinical. Liang Yue stood at the edge of the stainless steel table, her fingers pressed against the cold metal, trying to ground herself in its solidity.

The sheet covered everything. Everything that was left of Grandma.

"You don't have to do this," Professor Li said from behind her. His footsteps had stopped at the door, as if even he, with his thirty years of research and his leather journal and his careful scientific detachment, couldn't bring himself to approach the table.

"She's my grandmother." Liang Yue's voice came out flat. Hollow. "I'm not letting strangers be the last ones to see her face."

Her hands moved before she could think about it. The sheet came away like water, sliding over Grandma's features—the sharp cheekbones, the thin lips, the deep wrinkles that had always seemed like rivers on a map of some ancient country. She looked peaceful. That was the cruelest part. She looked like she was sleeping, like any moment she'd open her eyes and say *Yue'er, stop being so dramatic, I just needed a nap.*

But the skin was wrong. Too pale. Too still. And when Liang Yue touched her grandmother's cheek, the coldness sank into her bones like a living thing.

"The toxin," Professor Li said, moving closer now. "It's called Ghost Amber. A compound developed during the Cultural Revolution, when certain factions were trying to weaponize traditional medicine. It mimics the body's natural aging process, accelerating cellular death while leaving no trace—unless you know exactly what to look for."

Liang Yue's fingers traced down to Grandma's neck. There. Just below the jaw, a faint discoloration. She'd noticed it at the funeral home, but the director had said it was just bruising from the embalming process.

"Show me the test results again."

Professor Li spread the folder open on a nearby counter. "The compound binds to mitochondrial DNA. Standard toxicology screens don't detect it because it breaks down into harmless metabolites within forty-eight hours. But if you stain for the specific peptide chain it leaves behind—" He pointed at the liver cross-section, where a ghostly blue residue clung to the tissue like frost. "There. That's the marker."

Liang Yue's dantian burned. The furnace was pulsing now, a steady thrum that she'd come to recognize as warning. Or anger. She wasn't sure which anymore.

"Can you trace it?" she asked. "The source?"

"I've been trying for months. Ghost Amber requires a rare fungus that only grows in the bamboo forests of western Sichuan. The Zhang family controls most of those forests through shell companies." He paused. "But there's another possibility. Someone inside the university."

"Inside—" Liang Yue turned, her eyes narrowing. "What do you mean?"

"Your grandmother came to see me three days before she died. She was agitated. Said someone had been in her apartment, going through her research notes. She thought it was a student, at first. But then she found evidence of forced entry—a window lock that had been tampered with, a drawer that wasn't quite closed the way she'd left it."

"Why didn't she go to the police?"

"Because the police were already involved." Professor Li's voice dropped. "She'd received a visit from a Captain Zhao of the Chengdu Public Security Bureau. He told her that her work was being monitored for 'national security concerns.' Asked her about her connections to foreign researchers. She thought it was intimidation—a warning to stop her research."

Liang Yue's hands curled into fists. "The Zhang family owns the police."

"That's what I said. But there's something else." He pulled out another photograph—a security camera still from the university's main entrance. "This was taken the night your grandmother died. Look at the timestamp."

11:47 PM. Liang Yue squinted at the grainy image. A figure was entering the building, their face obscured by a hood. But the posture was familiar. The way they walked, shoulders slightly hunched, head tilted to one side—

"That's Dr. Hu," she breathed. "The associate dean of pharmaceutical sciences."

"Dr. Hu Xin. Your grandmother's former graduate student. And the man who recommended your scholarship application to this university."

The world tilted again. Dr. Hu had been a family friend for years. He'd come to Grandma's funeral, held Liang Yue's hand, told her that her grandmother would be proud of her. He'd even offered to help her with her coursework if she needed support.

"He's a Zhang," Professor Li said quietly. "Not by blood—he married into the family. But he's been their eyes and ears at this university for a decade. Your grandmother knew. She told me once that she'd trained him, trusted him, and that his betrayal was the deepest wound she'd ever carried."

"Why didn't she expose him?"

"Because exposure would have meant admitting she knew about the Zhang family's operations. And that would have put a target on her back even sooner." He shook his head. "She was trying to protect you. To keep you out of this until you were ready."

The furnace roared. Liang Yue felt it building in her chest, a pressure that demanded release. She'd spent years learning to control her emotions—pharmacy required precision, not passion. But this wasn't just emotion. This was something older, something that ran through her blood like fire.

"I'm going to kill him," she said. The words came out calm, measured. "I'm going to find Dr. Hu, and I'm going to make him tell me everything, and then I'm going to kill him."

"Liang Yue—"

"Don't." Her eyes snapped to Professor Li's face. "Don't tell me about the law. Don't tell me about evidence and due process. They murdered my grandmother. They used her own student to poison her, to steal her research, to—" Her voice cracked. She forced it steady. "I am the last Internal Alchemist. And I will not let them twist the Way into a weapon."

Professor Li was silent for a long moment. Then he reached into his jacket and pulled out a small metal case, no bigger than a phone. He set it on the counter between them.

"Your grandmother gave me this six years ago. She said I should only give it to you when you were ready to fight."

Liang Yue picked it up. The case was warm, almost alive, and when she pressed the latch, it opened to reveal a single brass needle. It was ancient, black with oxidation, and etched with characters so small she had to squint to read them.

*The needle that pierces the veil between life and death.*

"What is this?"

"A tool." Professor Li's voice was heavy. "For the final stage of Internal Alchemy—the refinement of the soul itself. Your grandmother believed that death is not the end, but a transformation. And that with the right preparation, the right intention, you can follow someone across that boundary."

Liang Yue stared at the needle. "You're talking about necromancy."

"I'm talking about truth." He met her eyes. "If you use this needle within twenty-four hours of death, while the soul still lingers near the body, you can ask one question. One question, and the dead will answer."

Her hand shook. The needle seemed to pulse in rhythm with the furnace, with her heartbeat, with the memory of Grandma's voice whispering in her ear.

"How long has it been?"

"Seventeen hours since your grandmother's official time of death. But the toxin accelerated her cellular decay—the soul may have already moved on."

"Or it may not have."

"Or it may not have." Professor Li's face was grim. "But there's a cost. Every use of the needle drains the user's Qi, weakens their connection to the physical world. Use it too many times, and you'll find yourself unable to return from the boundary."

Liang Yue looked at Grandma's face one last time. Peaceful. Still. Full of secrets she'd never had the chance to share.

She picked up the needle.

The moment it touched her skin, the world split. She was in the morgue, and she was somewhere else—a vast gray plain where the sky was the same color as the ground and nothing moved except the wind that didn't exist. Grandma stood before her, translucent, smiling.

*Yue'er. You came.*

"Grandma." The word tore out of her. "Who killed you?"

*The same people who will come for you. The Zhang family wants the furnace. They want the recipes. They want to turn alchemy into a factory—pills without soul, power without wisdom.*

"How do I stop them?"

Grandma's smile faded. *You can't stop them alone. But you're not alone. There are others—the old families, the ones who remember the true Way. Find them. Before the Zhangs do.*

"Where? How do I find them?"

*The needle.* Grandma's form was flickering, growing transparent. *The needle will guide you. But be careful, Yue'er. The path is narrow, and the darkness is—*

She stopped. Her eyes went wide, fixed on something behind Liang Yue.

*Run.*

The word hit like a physical force. Liang Yue turned—

And saw nothing. Just the gray plain, empty and silent.

When she opened her eyes, she was back in the morgue. The needle had fallen from her hand, clattering against the floor. Professor Li was staring at the door, his face pale.

"What—"

The door burst open.

Three men in black suits stood in the doorway, their faces blank. Behind them, a woman stepped forward, elegant and cold, her lips painted the color of dried blood.

"Miss Liang." Her voice was silk over steel. "So good to finally meet you. I'm Zhang Liling. And I believe you have something that belongs to my family."

The furnace screamed.

Liang Yue's hand closed around the brass needle.

"Your family killed my grandmother."

"Yes." Zhang Liling didn't even blink. "And we'll kill you too, unless you hand over the furnace. Right now."

The fluorescent lights flickered. Liang Yue felt the Qi building in her dantian, felt the furnace's fire spreading through her veins, felt the needle humming with ancient power.

"I have a better idea," she said.

Her voice was calm. Measured.

The way Grandma's had been, right before she'd burned her own research to keep it from falling into the wrong hands.

"Tell me where the other families are hiding. Or I'll burn your artificial Qi operation to the ground, with you inside it."

Zhang Liling laughed. It was a beautiful sound, like breaking glass.

"You think you can threaten me, little alchemist? You haven't even refined your third pill. You're barely more than a child playing with fire."

"Then come closer." Liang Yue raised the needle. "And find out what kind of fire I can make."

The morgue went silent. Everyone frozen in place.

Then Zhang Liling's smile vanished.

"Kill her," she said. "And take the furnace from her corpse."

The three men moved as one.

And Liang Yue learned that the dead grandmother she'd been conversing with had never been her grandmother at all—because the real Zhang family secret wasn't artificial Qi.

It was the ability to steal faces.

The woman who called herself Zhang Liling flickered, and for just a moment, Liang Yue saw Grandma's face staring back at her with dead, hungry eyes.

title: The Poison in the Blood
content:
Liang Yue stared at the stained liver section, her medical training cutting through the fog of grief. The dark patches weren't natural. They spiderwebbed through the tissue like black lightning frozen in amber.

"What am I looking at?" she asked, her voice steadier than she felt.

"Mercury sulfide nanoparticles," Professor Li said. "Engineered to bind to specific proteins in the liver. They accumulate slowly over months, then trigger catastrophic failure when a secondary compound is introduced."

"Like a trigger."

"Exactly. The primary poison was harmless alone. Your grandmother could have lived years with it in her system. But someone gave her the activator—probably in food or drink—and within hours, her liver shut down."

Liang Yue's mind raced. Grandma had been careful. She never ate out, never accepted food from strangers. She grew her own vegetables in the courtyard, cooked every meal herself.

Except—

*The tea ceremony. Three days before she died.*

Grandma's old friend from the Daoist association had visited. They'd drunk aged pu'er together, laughing about old times. Liang Yue had been in the other room, studying for finals, half-listening to their conversation.

*"The Zhang family has been asking about you,"* the friend had said. *"They want to revive the old collaboration."*

*"The old collaboration ended when my grandfather died,"* Grandma had replied. *"And it will stay ended."*

*"They say they've changed. That they only want to preserve the knowledge."*

*"They want to exploit it. There's a difference."*

Later that night, Grandma had seemed tired. Dizzy. She'd gone to bed early, and by morning, she was gone.

"Who was the friend?" Liang Yue asked abruptly.

Professor Li blinked. "What friend?"

"The one who visited her. Three days before she died. An old man, about her age, with a white beard. He wore a gray changshan and carried a walking stick with a jade handle."

"I don't know anyone matching that description." Professor Li's brow furrowed. "And I knew most of her contacts. She kept a list of everyone she trusted."

"She trusted him. She called him Brother Chen."

The professor's face went pale. "Chen? As in Chen Weimin?"

"I don't know his full name. She just said Brother Chen."

"Chen Weimin was her first student. Her most promising disciple." Professor Li's voice dropped. "He disappeared forty years ago. Everyone assumed he was dead."

"What happened to him?"

"He tried to synthesize Qi. To create a pill that could grant cultivation to anyone, regardless of natural talent. Your grandmother expelled him from the tradition when she discovered what he was doing. He swore revenge and vanished."

Liang Yue's blood ran cold. "You think he's working with the Zhang family?"

"I think he's been working with them for forty years." Professor Li pulled out his phone, scrolling through contacts. "Chen Weimin was a genius. If anyone could perfect artificial Qi, it would be him. And if the Zhang family funded his research, they'd have motive to eliminate anyone who stood in their way."

"Like Grandma."

"Like your grandmother. And like you, now that you have the furnace."

Liang Yue's hand went to her abdomen, where the furnace pulsed like a second heart. She could feel its heat, its ancient consciousness, its warning.

*Don't let them take it. Don't let them twist the Way.*

"I need to find Brother Chen," she said.

"That's suicide. If he's been hiding for forty years, he won't welcome a visit from the granddaughter of the woman who destroyed his life's work."

"Then I won't tell him who I am."

Professor Li shook his head. "He'll know. The furnace has a signature—a unique energy pattern that anyone trained in Internal Alchemy can sense. The moment you get within a hundred meters of him, he'll know exactly who you are."

"Then I need to mask the signature."

"That's impossible. The furnace is tied to your soul. You can't hide your soul."

Liang Yue thought of the pill recipes in Grandma's journal. The forbidden ones, the ones Grandma had marked with blood-red seals. *Consumption only in direst need. Side effects irreversible.*

There was one. The Shade Pills. Temporary cultivation suppression, designed to hide one's spiritual presence. They were used by ancient alchemists who needed to move unnoticed through hostile territories.

The side effect: temporary loss of all cultivation ability. For three days, the user would be as weak as any ordinary person. No enhanced senses. No accelerated healing. No protective Qi.

If she was caught during those three days, she'd be defenseless.

"Do it," she said.

Professor Li stared at her. "You understand what you're asking? If you take a Shade Pill, you lose everything. If the Zhang family finds you before the pill wears off—"

"They won't find me. Because I'll find Brother Chen first."

"You don't even know where he is."

"I know where he'll be." Liang Yue pulled out her phone, pulling up a map of Chengdu. "The Daoist association archives. Grandma said he spent years studying there as a young man. If he's back in Chengdu, he'll go there. To see what's changed."

"That's a guess."

"It's the only lead we have." She stood, her legs shaky but her resolve solid. "I'll take the pill tonight. Tomorrow morning, I'll be a normal college student visiting the archives for a research project. No one will look twice."

"And if Chen Weimin recognizes you anyway?"

"Then I'll deal with it." She met his eyes. "He killed my grandmother. He doesn't get to walk away."

Professor Li was silent for a long moment. Then he reached into his desk and pulled out a small wooden box, carved with the same alchemical symbols that decorated Grandma's furnace.

"Your grandmother gave me this, too. She said to give it to you when you were ready."

Liang Yue took the box, her hands trembling. Inside, nestled in red silk, lay a jade pendant carved in the shape of a phoenix. The stone was warm to the touch, pulsing with a faint light.

"What is it?"

"A protection talisman. Your grandmother spent three years charging it with her own Qi. It can absorb one fatal attack." He paused. "She made it after your grandfather died. She never wanted to lose anyone else."

Liang Yue fastened the pendant around her neck. The warmth spread through her chest, settling around her heart like a shield.

"I'll bring back answers," she said.

"Bring back yourself." Professor Li's voice cracked. "Your grandmother believed in you. She said you would be the one to restore the Way. Don't prove her wrong."

---

The Shade Pill tasted like ash and regret.

Liang Yue sat cross-legged in her dorm room, the last traces of the pill dissolving on her tongue. The furnace in her dantian flickered, protested, then went silent. She felt the loss like a missing limb—a hollow ache where power had once lived.

For the first time in months, she was ordinary.

She checked herself in the mirror. Same face. Same tired eyes. But the faint glow that had surrounded her since she'd awakened the furnace was gone. She looked like any other exhausted college student.

Good.

She pulled on a simple sweater and jeans, grabbed a canvas bag with a notebook and water bottle, and headed out. The morning air was crisp, the Sichuan mist clinging to the buildings like a veil.

The Daoist association archives were housed in an old temple on the outskirts of town, converted decades ago into a research library. The abbot, a wizened man with kind eyes, nodded as she entered.

"Student researcher?" he asked.

"Yes. I'm writing a paper on the history of Internal Alchemy in Sichuan province."

"Ah. A worthy topic." He gestured toward the back. "The historical records are in the eastern wing. Third floor, room 307."

She thanked him and climbed the creaking stairs. The archives smelled of old paper and incense, the air thick with centuries of accumulated knowledge. She found room 307 and slipped inside.

The room was empty—or so she thought.

Then a voice spoke from the shadows.

"You have her eyes."

Liang Yue froze. A figure emerged from behind a bookshelf, walking with the aid of a jade-handled cane.

Old. White beard. Gray changshan.

Brother Chen.

"You knew I'd come," she said, her voice steady.

"Of course." He smiled, but there was no warmth in it. "Your grandmother's granddaughter would never let a mystery lie. It's what made her great—and what got her killed."

"You killed her."

"I delivered the trigger. The Zhang family provided the poison. We're all culpable." He tapped his cane against the floor. "But you're here to avenge her, aren't you? To prove that the old ways are superior, that the furnace chose wisely."

"The furnace chose me for a reason."

"Yes. The same reason it chose your grandmother, and her grandmother before her." His eyes hardened. "Because the bloodline carries a unique resonance. A genetic predisposition to channel Qi. Not because of any virtue, but because of biology."

"That's not true."

"Isn't it?" He stepped closer. "Do you think you earned the furnace? You were born into it. Your grandmother handed it to you. You never had to struggle, to sacrifice, to prove yourself worthy."

"I've sacrificed plenty."

"Have you?" He laughed, a dry, bitter sound. "You don't know what sacrifice means. Your grandmother knew. She expelled me from the tradition because I refused to accept that enlightenment was reserved for the lucky few. Because I believed that anyone, anyone, could cultivate if given the right tools."

"By poisoning people?"

"I never poisoned anyone. The Zhang family's methods are crude, but their goal is noble—to democratize cultivation, to break the monopoly of the bloodlines." He spread his hands. "We could have worked together, you and I. Your grandmother's knowledge, my research, the Zhang family's resources. We could have changed the world."

"You killed my grandmother."

"A necessary sacrifice." His voice was cold. "She stood in the way of progress. Just as you stand in the way now."

Liang Yue's hand went to the phoenix pendant. It was warm, ready.

"I'm not standing in anyone's way," she said. "I'm here to finish what she started."

"And what was that?"

"She left me a message." Liang Yue reached into her bag, pulling out the leather journal. "A recipe. For a pill that can purify any poison, reverse any damage. Even damage caused by artificial Qi."

Chen Weimin's eyes widened. "That's impossible. No such pill exists."

"She invented it. In the last month of her life." Liang Yue opened the journal to the final page. "She knew she was dying. She knew you'd come for me. So she left me the weapon to stop you."

"The recipe is a lie."

"Is it?" Liang Yue held up the page, letting him see the intricate characters, the precise measurements, the alchemical symbols. "She wrote it in her own blood. The ultimate sacrifice—her life force, distilled into knowledge."

Chen Weimin's composure cracked. For a moment, Liang Yue saw the man he'd been forty years ago—the eager student, hungry for truth. Then the mask fell back into place.

"You're bluffing."

"Try me." She met his gaze, unblinking. "I have nothing left to lose. You took everything from me. But I still have the furnace, I still have her knowledge, and I still have the will to use it."

"You can't refine that pill. It requires ingredients

title: The Debt of Blood
content:
The morgue's fluorescent lights hummed with the sound of dying insects. Liang Yue stood over her grandmother's preserved body, the chemical smell of formaldehyde burning her nostrils, but she barely noticed. All she could see was the faint discoloration along Grandma's jawline—a purple bruising that the autopsy photos had only hinted at.

"Poison," she whispered. "They poisoned her."

Professor Li stood by the door, his hand resting on the lock. "Arsenic-based compound. Traces in her hair follicles, her bone marrow. Slow accumulation over three months. Whoever did this knew exactly what they were doing."

"Three months." Liang Yue's hands curled into fists. "She was dying for three months, and I didn't notice."

"You weren't supposed to notice. That's the point." He stepped closer, his voice dropping. "The Zhang family has alchemists of their own—not true Internal Alchemists, but practitioners of external methods. They know how to mask toxins, how to make death look natural."

"But you found it."

"Because I knew what to look for. Your grandmother taught me the signs." He paused. "She also taught me how to extract a confession."

Liang Yue turned sharply. "What do you mean?"

Professor Li reached into his jacket and pulled out a small glass vial, no larger than her thumb. Inside, a silver liquid swirled, catching the light like liquid mercury. "Elixir of Truth. One of your grandmother's recipes. Forces the drinker to speak only the truth for exactly seven minutes. No lies, no evasion, no control."

"Where did you get that?"

"She gave me three vials before she died. Said I'd know when to use them." He held it out to her. "There's a Zhang family operative in the university. Finance department. He's the one who processed the payments for Grandma's poisoning."

Liang Yue's blood ran hot. "You know who did it? And you didn't tell me?"

"Because I needed you to see her first. To understand what you're fighting for." His eyes were ancient, tired. "Revenge is a pill that's easy to swallow but impossible to digest. I wanted you to know the cost before you took the first dose."

"I'm not taking revenge." Liang Yue's voice was steel. "I'm taking justice. There's a difference."

"Is there?" He handed her the vial. "The elixir lasts seven minutes. Use them wisely."

---

Two hours later, Liang Yue sat in the university's finance office, watching a man named Chen Wei shuffle papers behind his desk. He was ordinary in every way—average height, average face, average posture. The kind of man who could disappear into any crowd.

"Miss Liang," he said, not looking up. "Your research grant application was denied. Insufficient academic merit."

"I'm not here about the grant."

"Then I'm afraid I don't have time for—"

"I'm here about my grandmother."

His hand paused mid-shuffle. For just a fraction of a second, his eyes flickered to her face. Then the mask was back. "I'm sorry for your loss. But I don't see how that concerns the finance department."

Liang Yue reached into her bag, pulling out a thermos. "I brought you tea. Oolong. Your favorite."

Chen Wei's eyes narrowed. "How do you know my favorite tea?"

"Grandma told me. She said you visited her often. Three months ago, when you started processing her payments."

"I don't—"

"Just drink the tea."

His hand moved toward the intercom. "I think you should leave before I call security."

"Call them." Liang Yue leaned back, crossing her arms. "By the time they get here, you'll have already drunk it. Or I'll pour it down your throat. Your choice."

Something flickered in his eyes—recognition, perhaps. The knowledge that this girl was not like the others. That she knew.

Slowly, reluctantly, he reached for the thermos. "If this is poisoned—"

"It's not. I'm not like you."

He drank. Three sips. Four. His throat bobbed as he swallowed.

For a moment, nothing happened. Then his eyes widened. His hands began to shake. The thermos clattered to the desk, spilling tea across the paperwork.

"What... what did you give me?"

"The truth." Liang Yue stood, walking around the desk until she was standing over him. "You have seven minutes. Every word you speak will be the complete, honest truth. You can't lie, you can't evade, you can't stay silent. So let's start with an easy one. Did you poison my grandmother?"

"Yes." The word came out like a gunshot, and his face twisted in horror. "I didn't want to. But they had my daughter. They said if I didn't help, they'd take her."

"Who is 'they'?"

"The Zhang family. The third branch. Zhang Wei's people."

"And what did you do?"

"I processed the payments. I made sure the alchemists who prepared the toxin got their money. I falsified records to hide the transfers."

"How many payments?"

"Twelve. Over three months. Total of 2.4 million yuan."

Liang Yue's stomach turned. "And my grandmother's death?"

"That was the final payment. I was told to expect it. I didn't know when, only that it would happen."

"Who gave the final order?"

"Zhang Wei personally. He said the old woman had outlived her usefulness. That the furnace needed a new holder."

"The furnace. Why does he want it?"

"Because it's the key." Chen Wei's voice cracked. "The Zhang family has been trying to create artificial Qi for decades. But every attempt fails because they can't replicate the furnace's ability to refine spiritual energy. Your grandmother's furnace—it's the last one that works. The last one that can produce true Internal Alchemy pills."

"And they think if they have it, they can figure out how to make more?"

"They don't want to make more. They want to destroy it. The furnace is proof that Internal Alchemy exists, that the old ways are valid. The Zhang family wants to erase that proof. They want to be the only source of alchemical knowledge in China."

Liang Yue's blood ran cold. "They want to monopolize alchemy."

"More than that. They want to control it. Control who has access to healing pills, to longevity formulas, to power." Chen Wei's eyes were desperate now. "They're building an empire, Miss Liang. And you're standing in their way."

"How many operatives does the Zhang family have in this university?"

"Seventeen. I'm not the only one. There are professors, staff, students. We watch. We report. We act when ordered."

"Name them."

His mouth opened, but no words came out. His face contorted, veins bulging in his neck. "I... I can't..."

"Why can't you?"

"Because... because they implanted something in me. A failsafe. If I try to reveal the network, it..."

His body went rigid. His eyes rolled back. Foam bubbled at his lips.

"No!" Liang Yue grabbed his shoulders. "Chen Wei! Stay with me!"

But his body was already seizing, his back arching off the chair. The silver liquid in his blood—the truth elixir—was reacting to something. Something inside him.

Professor Li burst through the door. "What happened?"

"He was about to name names and then—"

"Then the failsafe activated." Li rushed to Chen Wei's side, pulling out a small case. "I should have anticipated this. The Zhang family doesn't leave loose ends."

"Is he going to die?"

"That depends on how fast I can work." Li opened the case, revealing rows of acupuncture needles. "Hold him steady."

Liang Yue pinned Chen Wei's shoulders as Li inserted needles into his neck, his temples, the crown of his head. The convulsions slowed, then stopped. Chen Wei's breathing became shallow, but steady.

"He'll live," Li said. "But he won't remember anything from the last hour. The failsafe erases short-term memory to protect the network."

"So we got nothing."

"Not nothing. We confirmed the Zhang family's involvement. We know they're after the furnace. And we know they have seventeen operatives in this university." He looked at her. "Which means we have a traitor problem."

Liang Yue's phone buzzed. A message from an unknown number.

*The body in the morgue is not your grandmother. Come to the Night Market. Alone. Come before midnight, or the truth dies with me.*

She showed it to Li. His face went pale.

"It's a trap."

"Probably."

"Then you're not going."

"I have to." She pocketed her phone. "If there's even a chance that Grandma's still alive..."

"Liang Yue." He grabbed her arm. "Think. If the Zhang family has her—if she's alive—why would they wait until now to tell you? Why not use her as leverage immediately?"

"Because they didn't know I'd found the poison. They thought I'd accept the heart attack story and move on. Now that I know the truth, they need to control the narrative."

"And you walking into a trap is exactly what they want."

"I know." She pulled free of his grip. "But I'm going anyway. Because if Grandma is out there, suffering, thinking I abandoned her..."

"Then take backup."

"No. They said alone. If I bring anyone, they'll kill her."

"Liang Yue—"

"Trust me." She met his eyes. "I'm the last Internal Alchemist. I didn't survive this long by being stupid."

She walked out of the office, the furnace pulsing in her dantian like a second heart. The Night Market waited. The Zhang family waited. And somewhere, if the message was true, her grandmother waited too.

The fluorescent lights flickered as she passed, casting long shadows that danced like hungry ghosts.

Midnight was three hours away.

And the night had only just begun.

title: The Poison in the Blood
content:
Liang Yue stared at the image. The chemical marker had turned Grandma's liver cells a sickly green—the color of decay, of something unnatural. She'd seen enough histology slides in her pharmacy courses to know what healthy tissue looked like. This wasn't it.

"What am I looking at?" she asked, though a cold certainty was already settling in her chest.

"Bismuth subgallate. Combined with a synthetic compound I've never seen before." Professor Li pointed to a cluster of dark spots on the scan. "These aren't natural metabolic byproducts. They're crystalline formations. The toxin was designed to accumulate over months, gradually disrupting cellular respiration until—"

"Until her heart stopped." Liang Yue's voice came out flat, robotic. She'd learned this in pharmacology too. The difference between acute poisoning and chronic accumulation. Grandma hadn't been killed in a single moment. She'd been killed slowly, methodically, over half a year.

"Six months," Professor Li confirmed, as if reading her thoughts. "The half-life of the compound suggests initial exposure around New Year's."

New Year's. Liang Yue's mind raced back. Grandma had been fine at New Year's. They'd made tangyuan together, the sweet rice balls filled with black sesame paste. Grandma had laughed at Liang Yue's clumsy wrapping, had shown her how to pinch the dough just so—

"The furnace," she said suddenly. "They were trying to get to the furnace. If they poisoned her slowly, they must have been watching her, waiting for her to give it up."

"Or waiting for her to die naturally so they could search her house without suspicion." Professor Li's jaw tightened. "But she outsmarted them. She gave it to you before the toxin could finish its work."

Liang Yue's hand went to her dantian instinctively. The furnace pulsed there, warm and alive, like a second heart. *Don't let them twist the Way.* Grandma's last words made terrible sense now.

"I need to see her," Liang Yue said. "The body. I need to—"

"Absolutely not." Professor Li's voice was sharp. "If anyone sees you there, if anyone connects you to her death—"

"I don't care." Liang Yue stood up, her chair scraping against the floor. "She's my grandmother. She raised me. She died because she was protecting this—" She pressed her hand against her abdomen. "And I've been sitting in my dorm room refining pills like nothing happened."

"Liang Yue—"

"She taught me everything, Professor Li. Everything except what was really happening." Her voice cracked. "And now I have to figure it out alone."

"You're not alone." He stood too, moving around the desk. "I've been protecting her research for three decades. I'm not about to stop now."

"Then take me to the morgue."

He studied her for a long moment, his tired eyes searching hers. Whatever he saw there made him nod slowly.

"Tonight. After midnight. The night shift guard is a friend."

---

The university morgue was in the basement of the medical school, three floors below ground. The corridors were empty at 12:30 AM, the fluorescent lights humming with a sickly buzz. Liang Yue followed Professor Li through a maze of hallways, her sneakers squeaking on the linoleum.

"Why midnight?" she asked, keeping her voice low.

"Fewer cameras. Fewer questions." He stopped at a steel door, punched in a code. "And the morgue attendant owes me a favor."

The door swung open into a cold room that smelled of formaldehyde and bleach. Steel tables lined the walls, most of them empty. But one, near the back, held a sheet-covered figure.

Liang Yue's breath caught.

"Are you sure you want to do this?" Professor Li asked softly.

She didn't answer. She walked forward, her legs moving on their own, until she stood beside the table. Her hand reached out, trembling, and pulled back the sheet.

Grandma looked... peaceful. That was the worst part. Her skin was waxy, her lips slightly blue, but her face was relaxed, almost smiling. Like she'd simply fallen asleep.

But Liang Yue knew better now.

She pulled out the small brass pill furnace she'd brought—not the true furnace in her dantian, but a decoy she'd refined from copper and her own Qi. It was crude, barely functional, but it had one property that mattered: it could detect Qi residue.

She pressed the furnace against Grandma's chest, right over the heart.

For a moment, nothing.

Then the furnace began to glow—a sickly green light that pulsed in rhythm with Liang Yue's own heartbeat.

"What does that mean?" Professor Li asked, leaning closer.

"It means there's still Qi residue in her body. The poison wasn't just chemical. It was alchemical." Liang Yue's voice was hollow. "They used a pill. A modified version of the Dispersing Breath Pill."

"The one you told me about? That purges spiritual energy?"

"A corrupted version." She pulled the furnace away, but the green light lingered in the air for a moment before fading. "The original pill helps cultivators expel impurities. This one was designed to do the opposite—to bind Qi to the cells and prevent the body from healing itself."

"Making her death look natural while slowly suffocating her spiritual core."

"Yes." Liang Yue's hands clenched into fists. "Only an Internal Alchemist could have made this pill. Someone who knew the original formula."

Professor Li's face went pale. "You think there's another alchemist? Working for the Zhang family?"

"I think there's a traitor." Liang Yue turned to face him, her eyes hard. "Grandma was the last Internal Alchemist. That's what she always told me. But someone else knows the recipes. Someone else has been practicing the Way."

"But if there's another alchemist, why did they need Grandma's furnace?"

"Because the furnace isn't just a tool. It's a key." She looked down at Grandma's face, memorizing every line, every wrinkle. "The true furnace stores not just Qi, but knowledge. Generations of alchemical secrets, passed down through bloodlines. Whoever made this poison pill had partial knowledge. But they need the furnace to access the complete records."

"Which means they need you."

"They need me alive," Liang Yue corrected. "At least until they can extract the furnace's knowledge."

She reached out and touched Grandma's cheek. The skin was cold, stiff. But beneath it, she could feel something else—a faint warmth, like embers dying in a hearth.

Grandma's Qi. Fading, but not gone.

"I can save her," Liang Yue whispered.

"What?"

"Her Qi is still there. Faint, but present. If I can refine it, purify it, I might be able to—"

"Liang Yue, she's dead. Her body has been dead for three weeks."

"No. Her body is dead. But her spirit—" She pressed both hands against Grandma's chest, closing her eyes. "In Internal Alchemy, death isn't the end. It's a transition. If I can gather her remaining Qi, I can preserve it. Keep it safe until I find a way to—"

"To what? Bring her back?" Professor Li's voice was sharp with concern. "That's not alchemy. That's necromancy."

"It's the Way." Liang Yue opened her eyes. "The Way is transformation. Change. Grandma always said that matter and spirit are the same thing, just vibrating at different frequencies. If I can learn to shift that frequency..."

"This is dangerous. You're talking about crossing lines that alchemists have warned against for centuries."

"I know." She looked at Grandma's face one last time, then pulled the sheet back over it. "But I'm not going to let her die for nothing."

She placed the decoy furnace on Grandma's chest, right over the heart. Then she began to chant—soft, ancient syllables that Grandma had taught her in whispers, late at night, when the world was sleeping.

*The fire refines. The water cleanses. The spirit rises from the mud.*

The furnace began to glow again, but this time the light was golden, warm. It spread from the furnace to Grandma's body, tracing the lines of her veins, her arteries, her meridians. The green Qi residue began to burn away, dissolving into nothing.

And beneath it, something stirred.

A spark. Tiny, fragile, but undeniably present.

Liang Yue grabbed it with her own Qi, pulling it into the furnace, cradling it like a newborn flame.

"Got you," she breathed.

"Liang Yue!" Professor Li's voice was urgent now. "We need to go. Someone's coming."

She looked up. Through the morgue's frosted glass door, she could see shadows moving. Multiple figures. And the green glow of Qi—not pure like hers, but tainted, artificial.

The Zhang family's men.

"Go," she said, shoving the furnace into her bag. "Now."

But as they reached the door, it burst open.

A man stood in the doorway. Tall, sharp-featured, with eyes that burned like cold fire. He wore a suit that cost more than Liang Yue's entire year's tuition, and his Qi reeked of chemicals and synthetic power.

"Miss Liang," he said, his voice smooth as silk. "We've been looking for you."

Behind him, four more figures emerged from the shadows. Their eyes glowed green.

Professor Li stepped in front of her. "Zhang Wei. I should have known."

"Professor Li." The man's smile was razor-thin. "Still meddling in matters you don't understand. I thought you'd learned your lesson after the last time."

"Last time?" Liang Yue's voice was ice.

"Your grandmother's first warning," Zhang Wei said. "A car accident that nearly killed him. We thought he'd take the hint."

Liang Yue's vision went red.

*You.* The word burned in her throat. *You tried to kill him too.*

"We're done talking." Zhang Wei raised his hand, and the four figures behind him surged forward.

Liang Yue didn't think. She reached into her bag, pulled out a handful of pills—the ones she'd refined in her dorm room, the ones she'd made from Grandma's recipes.

She crushed them in her palm.

The powder ignited, filling the corridor with blinding white light and the scent of sandalwood. The figures recoiled, their artificial Qi flickering.

"Run!" Liang Yue grabbed Professor Li's arm and pulled him through a side door.

They burst into the stairwell, taking the steps two at a time. Above them, they could hear shouts, footsteps, the crackle of corrupted Qi.

"Where are we going?" Professor Li gasped.

"The roof." Liang Yue's mind was racing. "I have a plan."

"A plan? What plan?"

She didn't answer. She just ran.

Up and up, past the ground floor, past the second, the third. The roof door was locked, but Liang Yue pressed her hand against it, channeling her Qi into the lock mechanism. It clicked open.

They burst out onto the rooftop. The night air was cold, the city lights glittering below them.

"Now what?" Professor Li asked.

Liang Yue turned to face the door. The stairwell was filling with green light.

"Now," she said, pulling out the decoy furnace, "we see if Grandma's teachings were right."

She threw the furnace onto the ground. It cracked open, spilling golden light across the rooftop.

And then she waited.

Zhang Wei emerged first, his green eyes gleaming. "A brave attempt, Miss Liang. But futile."

"You want the furnace?" Liang Yue pointed at the broken decoy.

title: The Poison in the Blood
content:
The morgue's fluorescent lights hummed like trapped insects, casting everything in a sickly green pallor. Liang Yue stood over her grandmother's preserved remains, her fingers pressed against the cold metal table's edge until her knuckles went white.

"It doesn't match," Professor Li said, pointing at the toxicology report. "The toxin they found—aconitine, from monkshood—that would cause cardiac arrest within hours. But your grandmother's symptoms lasted weeks."

Liang Yue forced herself to study the images. Grandma's liver, cross-sectioned and stained, showed patterns she'd only seen in pharmacology textbooks. "This isn't aconitine poisoning. The cellular damage is wrong."

"Exactly." Professor Li pulled out another sheet. "I ran a secondary panel. What killed your grandmother was a compound I've never seen before. It's not in any database. But look at the molecular structure."

He handed her the chemical diagram. Liang Yue's breath caught. The structure was beautiful—elegant rings of carbon and nitrogen arranged in a pattern that seemed almost alive. It reminded her of something.

"The Furnace Records," she whispered. "Grandma had a book. Ancient recipes for pills that could..." She trailed off, the implication crashing down on her.

"Pills that could kill," Professor Li finished. "And leave no trace. Until someone knew what to look for."

Liang Yue's phone buzzed. She ignored it.

"This compound requires alchemical refinement," she said slowly. "Modern chemistry can't produce these ring structures. The bond angles are impossible without Qi manipulation."

"Which means whoever killed your grandmother is an alchemist."

"No." Liang Yue shook her head. "Not an alchemist. An Internal Alchemist. Or someone who's studied the Furnace Records closely enough to replicate the techniques."

The morgue's door creaked. Both of them turned. A janitor stood there, mop in hand, his face blank and unremarkable.

"Sorry," he mumbled. "Didn't know anyone was down here."

He shuffled away. Liang Yue watched him go, a cold feeling settling in her stomach.

"We need to move the body," she said. "Somewhere safe."

"Where?"

She thought of Grandma's apartment, still sealed with police tape. Of the temple in the mountains where Grandma had taken her as a child. Of the furnace pulsing in her dantian, warm and insistent.

"The pharmacology lab," she said. "Third floor. No windows, reinforced door. I know the access codes."

Professor Li nodded. "Help me with the gurney."

They worked quickly, transferring the preserved remains into a sealed container. Liang Yue's hands moved with practiced efficiency, but her mind raced. The Zhang family. Artificial Qi. A compound that mimicked natural death. The pieces were there, but they didn't quite fit.

"Why would they kill her?" she asked as they wheeled the gurney through empty hallways. "If they wanted the furnace, why not just take it?"

"Because the furnace chooses its master." Professor Li's voice was grim. "Without the bloodline, without the correct cultivation technique, it's just a brass pot. Worthless to anyone but the true successor."

"Then killing Grandma didn't help them."

"No. But it might have been a test." He stopped at the elevator, pressed the button. "To see if the succession would trigger. To see if the furnace would reveal itself."

The elevator doors opened. Inside stood three men in expensive suits. The one in front smiled, his teeth too white, his eyes too cold.

"Professor Li," he said. "What a coincidence. And you must be Liang Yue. We've been looking for you."

Liang Yue's hand moved instinctively to her dantian. The furnace flared, hot and warning.

"Who are you?" she asked.

"Zhang Wei." He extended a hand. She didn't take it. "I'm the family's head of research. We have a mutual interest in alchemy, I believe."

"I don't know what you're talking about."

"Of course you don't." His smile didn't waver. "But I think you'll find this interesting." He pulled out a small vial from his jacket, filled with a golden liquid that seemed to glow from within. "This is our latest breakthrough. A Qi supplement that can be administered to anyone. No cultivation required."

Professor Li stepped forward. "You're poisoning people."

"We're advancing humanity." Zhang Wei's eyes flickered with something like hunger. "Why should enlightenment be limited to those born with the talent? Why should ancient families hoard power when it could be synthesized, distributed, democratized?"

Liang Yue stared at the vial. The liquid moved strangely, almost alive, forming patterns that shifted and reformed.

"It's unstable," she said.

"Excuse me?"

"The Qi you've trapped in there. It's not balanced. The five elements are fighting each other." She could feel it now, the chaotic energy writhing inside the glass. "Whoever takes that will experience a temporary boost, then their system will collapse. Organ failure within a week."

Zhang Wei's smile faltered. "That's impossible. Our scientists—"

"Your scientists don't understand Qi." Liang Yue met his eyes. "They're trying to force a square peg through a round hole. And people are dying."

The elevator's doors started to close. Zhang Wei caught them with his hand.

"You're very confident for someone who's been an alchemist for less than a month," he said softly. "But you're right. Our formula is incomplete. Which is why we need your grandmother's research."

"She didn't leave any research."

"Everyone leaves research." He stepped closer. "And everyone has a price. What's yours? Money? Status? A cure for your roommate's cancer?"

The words hit like a slap. "How do you know about Sun Yi?"

"We know everything, Miss Liang. The furnace. The professor. The roommate who's been getting sicker every day." His smile returned. "We have people everywhere. Including this hospital's oncology department."

Liang Yue's blood turned to ice. Sun Yi had been her roommate for three years. Quiet, studious, always reading romance novels when she should be studying. Six months ago, she'd been diagnosed with stage four pancreatic cancer. The doctors gave her six months. She was on month five.

"You're lying."

"Am I? Check her medical records. The Zhang Foundation has been covering her treatment costs. Did you never wonder why a broke student could afford experimental therapies?"

The furnace roared in Liang Yue's chest. She wanted to hit him. She wanted to reach into his chest and tear out whatever passed for a heart.

But she held still.

"What do you want?" she asked, her voice flat.

"The Furnace Records. All of them. In exchange, we'll save your friend. We have treatments—real treatments, not the garbage we show the public. We can give her another twenty years."

"And if I refuse?"

Zhang Wei's eyes went cold. "Then the next treatment she receives will be one of our experimental Qi supplements. I hear the side effects are... impressive."

He stepped back into the elevator. The doors slid closed.

Professor Li grabbed her arm. "Liang Yue, you can't—"

"I know." She pulled away. "But I also can't let Sun Yi die."

"What about the furnace? What about everything your grandmother taught you?"

Liang Yue looked at the container holding Grandma's remains. At the photos tucked under Professor Li's arm. At the vial she could still feel, pulsing with poisoned light.

"Grandma taught me that alchemy is about balance," she said quietly. "Life and death. Creation and destruction. The pill that heals and the pill that kills." She turned to face him. "And she taught me that sometimes, the only way to win is to let your enemy think they've already won."

"What are you planning?"

"I'm going to give them what they want." Her voice hardened. "With a few modifications of my own."

Professor Li's face paled. "You're going to poison them."

"I'm going to balance the scales." She pulled out her phone, finally checking the missed call. It was from the hospital. Sun Yi's room number.

Her hands were steady as she dialed.

"Hello? This is Liang Yue. I need to schedule a visit. My roommate, Sun Yi. Room 417." She paused. "And I need to speak with someone from the Zhang Foundation. I have a proposal they might be interested in."

The nurse on the other end sounded confused. "Miss, the Zhang Foundation representative just left. He said you'd be calling."

Of course he did.

Liang Yue hung up. The furnace pulsed once, twice, a heartbeat of ancient fire. She could feel Grandma's presence, a whisper at the edge of her consciousness.

*The Way cannot be found through science alone, nor through spirit alone.*

"Professor," she said, "I need access to the university's chemical synthesis lab. And I need you to trust me."

"With what?"

"With everything."

He studied her for a long moment. Then he nodded.

"Third floor. Room 312. I'll clear it with security."

"No," Liang Yue said. "Don't clear it with anyone. Not anymore."

She walked toward the stairs, the container holding Grandma's remains heavy in her hands. Behind her, the elevator hummed, carrying the Zhang family's poison deeper into the hospital's veins.

She had three days until Sun Yi's next treatment.

Three days to create a pill that could save her friend.

And three days to make sure the Zhang family never hurt anyone again.

The furnace burned hot in her chest.

It was time to remind the world why Internal Alchemists were feared.

title: The Poison in the Roots
content:
The chemical marker in Grandma's liver glowed like a constellation of betrayal under the microscope's blue light. Liang Yue's hand trembled as she adjusted the focus, her pharmacology training screaming the diagnosis before her conscious mind could catch up.

"Rare earth metal accumulation," she whispered. "Lanthanides."

Professor Li nodded slowly. "Four different types. Cerium, neodymium, samarium, and gadolinium. Deposited over approximately six months. Each dose too small to trigger immediate symptoms."

"But they accumulate." Liang Yue's voice cracked. "They bind to calcium channels in the heart muscle. Cause arrhythmias. Mimic heart failure."

"Your grandmother was dying by millimeters, and she knew it." Professor Li pulled out another document—a handwritten letter, the paper yellowed with age. "She left this for you. In my care. To be given 'when my granddaughter's eyes show the fire of understanding, not the fog of grief.'"

Liang Yue took the letter with hands that felt like they belonged to someone else. Grandma's seal—a phoenix clutching a pill furnace—gleamed in crimson wax. She broke it open.

*My dearest Yue'er,*

*If you're reading this, you've found Li Wei. And you've seen the truth I couldn't speak aloud.*

*The Zhang family killed me. Not out of cruelty, but out of desperation. Their artificial Qi project has failed. Repeatedly. The human test subjects—there were seventeen of them, Yue'er, seventeen souls who trusted them—all died. Not from the Qi injections themselves, but from what the Qi couldn't do.*

*You see, artificial Qi can mimic the power. It can strengthen muscles, sharpen senses, extend lifespans. But it cannot refine the soul. And without soul refinement, the body eventually rejects the energy. The subjects went mad. Their Qi turned against them, consuming them from within.*

*The Zhang family knows the only solution lies in the Internal Alchemy furnace—the true furnace, passed down through our bloodline for eight hundred years. They need the Pill of Soul Harmony. The recipe exists only in our family's secret texts, the ones I burned before they could find them.*

*I memorized them. And now, so must you.*

Liang Yue's breath caught. She read faster.

*The Pill of Soul Harmony requires three impossible ingredients:*
1. *The marrow of a phoenix bone*
2. *The tears of a bodhisattva*
3. *The heartblood of an Internal Alchemist who has reached the Ninth Transformation*

*I reached the Seventh Transformation before I died. You, my darling granddaughter, are at the First Transformation—barely. You have years of cultivation ahead before you can even attempt such a pill.*

*But the Zhang family cannot wait. Their financial empire is collapsing. Their investors are demanding results. They will come for you, and they will come soon.*

*So I have left you something else. A shorter path. A dangerous one.*

*Beneath the Wenshu Monastery, there is a chamber sealed since the Tang Dynasty. It contains the remains of an Internal Alchemist who reached the Ninth Transformation—the last one to ever achieve it. Her name was Jingwei. She died in 845 AD, during the Great Buddhist Persecution.*

*Her heartblood still beats in a crystal vial, preserved by her final alchemical act.*

*Take it. Use it. But know this: Jingwei was not a good woman. She was a demon-suppressing cultivator who slaughtered thousands in the name of purification. Her heartblood carries her karma. If you consume it, you will inherit not just her power, but her debts.*

*The choice is yours. The Way has always been about choices.*

*I love you. I am proud of you. And I am sorry I couldn't be there to guide you further.*

*—Grandma*

Liang Yue folded the letter, her knuckles white. Professor Li watched her with the patience of a man who had already read its contents.

"The Wenshu Monastery," she said. "That's where Grandma used to take me for temple fairs. We'd light incense and pray for my exams."

"The monastery sits on a network of underground chambers dating back to the Three Kingdoms period. The Buddhist association knows about them, but they've sealed most of them off. Dangerous energy, they say. Unstable Qi."

"Demons."

"Or what's left of them." Professor Li's voice dropped. "Jingwei wasn't just an Internal Alchemist. She was a demon hunter. She purified Chengdu of an entire nest of Yasha-class demons in 840 AD. The legends say she didn't just kill them—she refined their souls into pills."

Liang Yue's stomach turned. "That's... that's forbidden. The Way of Internal Alchemy forbids using sentient souls as ingredients."

"The Way as your grandmother taught it. But Jingwei followed the Old Way—the way of the Warring States period, when Internal Alchemists were warlords and their pills were weapons." Professor Li leaned forward. "There's a reason the Zhang family hasn't found the recipe for Soul Harmony. It's not in any text. It was never written down."

"Then how—"

"Because Jingwei is the only one who ever created it. And she took the recipe to her grave."

Liang Yue's phone buzzed. A text from Chen Wei: *Someone's asking about you at the pharmacy. Stranger. Wearing a very expensive suit. I told them you were sick. You might want to lie low.*

She showed the message to Professor Li. His face went pale.

"They're already here. Faster than I expected." He stood abruptly, gathering the documents. "We need to move. Now."

"Where?"

"The monastery. If they find you before you get to the chamber, everything your grandmother sacrificed will be for nothing."

They exited through the back of the biology building, cutting through a maintenance corridor that smelled of formaldehyde and bleach. Professor Li moved with surprising speed for a man his age, his cane tapping a steady rhythm against the concrete floor.

"How do we get into the chamber?" Liang Yue asked, her backpack heavy with the furnace and Grandma's journal. "I doubt the monks will just let us dig up their basement."

"The Seal of the Nine Heavens. Your grandmother gave it to me years ago, along with the journal." He pulled a small jade pendant from beneath his shirt—a circular disk carved with interlocking dragons. "This is the key. It resonates with Jingwei's alchemical signature."

"And the monks?"

"Are old friends. Your grandmother saved the abbot's life thirty years ago. He owes her a debt."

They emerged into the alley behind the university, the afternoon sun harsh after the dim corridor. Chengdu's perpetual haze hung over the buildings, turning the light a sickly yellow. Liang Yue pulled her hood up, scanning the street.

Two men in black suits stood at the corner, their postures too rigid, their gazes too sharp. They weren't looking at her—not yet—but they were clearly waiting.

"Zhang family enforcers," Professor Li muttered. "Qi cultivators. Low level, but dangerous in numbers."

"How did they find us so fast?"

"Your grandmother's death created ripples in the supernatural community. Every faction in Chengdu knows the last Internal Alchemist has awakened. The Zhang family has been preparing for this moment for years."

They walked quickly, blending into the crowd of students and street vendors. The enforcers didn't follow—not immediately—but Liang Yue felt their gazes like needles on her back.

The Wenshu Monastery was a twenty-minute walk through the heart of Chengdu's old city. They passed through the Liulichang antique district, past shops selling jade Buddhas and Mao-era propaganda posters, past street food stalls where the scent of mapo tofu mixed with exhaust fumes.

Liang Yue's phone buzzed again. Chen Wei: *They're outside my dorm. Asking about you. I told them you went to Chongqing for the weekend. They didn't believe me.*

She typed back: *Stay safe. Don't follow me. I'll explain everything later.*

Chen Wei: *Like hell I won't follow you. You're my best friend and you're clearly in trouble. Where are you?*

Liang Yue hesitated. Then she typed: *Wenshu Monastery. Don't come. Please.*

No response.

The monastery's red walls rose before them, ancient and serene, the curved rooflines adorned with ceramic guardians. Incense smoke curled from the main hall, carrying prayers on its tendrils. Monks in grey robes swept the courtyard with bamboo brooms, their movements meditative.

An elderly monk approached them, his face weathered like old leather. He bowed to Professor Li, then turned his eyes to Liang Yue. They were the clearest eyes she'd ever seen—like polished obsidian, reflecting nothing and everything.

"You have the seal," he said. It wasn't a question.

"I do, Abbot Xuan." Professor Li handed over the jade pendant.

The abbot examined it, then nodded. "The chamber has been waiting. There have been... disturbances lately. The old energy stirring. We thought it was seismic activity, but now I understand."

"What kind of disturbances?" Liang Yue asked.

"Dreams. The monks have been dreaming of a woman in white armor, wielding a sword of light. She speaks of a war that never ended." The abbot's eyes met hers. "Jingwei's spirit is restless. She knows someone is coming to claim her inheritance."

"Is she dangerous?"

"The old Internal Alchemists were not like your grandmother, child. They were forged in an age of war and demons. They did not heal—they destroyed." He paused. "Jingwei killed three thousand demons in her lifetime. And she enjoyed it."

Liang Yue's hand went to the furnace, warm against her chest. "I don't want to be like her. I just want to stop the Zhang family."

"The path to power is rarely the path you choose. Sometimes, the path chooses you." The abbot turned, gesturing for them to follow. "Come. The entrance is beneath the Hall of the Heavenly Kings."

They walked through the monastery, past halls filled with gilded Buddhas and painted bodhisattvas, past gardens where koi fish swam in circles beneath lotus leaves. The atmosphere changed as they approached the Hall of the Heavenly Kings—the air grew heavier, charged with something that made Liang Yue's skin prickle.

The abbot stopped before a stone altar dedicated to Virūpākṣa, the Heavenly King of the West. He pressed a series of carvings on the altar's surface, and with a grinding sound, the floor beneath them split open, revealing a spiral staircase descending into darkness.

"The chamber is three levels down," the abbot said. "The seal will open the final door. But be warned—Jingwei's residual Qi permeates the space. It may try to test you."

"Test me how?"

"By showing you your deepest fear. Your greatest weakness. The thing you hate most about yourself." The abbot's eyes were kind, but his voice was grim. "The old cultivators believed that only those who had confronted their own darkness could wield true power."

Liang Yue took a breath. She thought of Grandma, dying slowly from poison. She thought of the seventeen test subjects, their souls consumed by artificial Qi. She thought of the Zhang family, closing in like wolves.

"I'm ready."

She descended into the darkness, Professor Li following close behind. The staircase spiraled down, the stone walls slick with moisture, the air growing colder with each step. The furnace pulsed in her dantian, its warmth a comfort against the chill.

At the bottom, a massive bronze door loomed before them, covered in Tao

title: The Poison That Lingers
content:
The chemical marker in Grandma’s liver glowed under the microscope’s ultraviolet light like a constellation of dying stars. Liang Yue pressed her eye to the eyepiece, her breath fogging the lens.

“What am I looking at?” she asked, her voice flat.

Professor Li adjusted the focus. “That’s a heavy metal compound. Synthetic. I’ve never seen anything like it in nature. It binds to mitochondrial DNA and triggers apoptosis—programmed cell death—over approximately six weeks.”

“Six weeks.” Liang Yue pulled back, her hands gripping the edge of the examination table. “She was sick for six weeks. The doctors said it was old age. Her heart just... giving out.”

“Old age doesn’t leave this pattern.” He pointed to another image—a heat map of Grandma’s neural tissue. “The compound also accumulates in the brain. It causes confusion, memory lapses, emotional instability. Symptoms that look like dementia.”

Liang Yue’s stomach turned. She remembered Grandma’s last month. The way she’d forget where she put her glasses. The way she’d stare at Liang Yue sometimes with blank, uncomprehending eyes. The way she’d scream in the middle of the night, calling for her dead husband, her dead son, anyone who’d already crossed the river.

“They poisoned her slowly,” Liang Yue said. The words tasted like ash. “They watched her fall apart.”

“Yes.”

“And they made it look natural.”

“Yes.”

Liang Yue’s hands balled into fists. The furnace in her dantian blazed, sending tendrils of heat up her spine. Her vision flickered—for a moment, she saw the room through a haze of gold and crimson, the Qi signatures of everything around her glowing like fireflies.

Professor Li’s Qi was steady, measured. Blue-green, like deep water. Honest.

But somewhere outside this room, someone’s Qi was tainted. Dark. Poisoned by the same compound that had killed Grandma.

“The Zhang family,” Liang Yue said. “Who did it? Which one?”

“I don’t know.” Professor Li’s voice was tired. “I’ve been trying to trace the compound’s origin for months. It’s not commercially available. It has to be custom-synthesized. That requires a lab, equipment, and someone with advanced knowledge of both chemistry and internal alchemy.”

“Internal alchemy?” Liang Yue’s head snapped up. “You think an alchemist made that poison?”

“I think someone who studied alchemy made it. Someone who knew enough to weaponize the principles without understanding the Way.” He paused. “Someone like your grandmother’s former disciple.”

The words hung in the air like a guillotine blade.

“What former disciple?” Liang Yue asked, though she already knew the answer.

“Before you were born, your grandmother took on a student. A young man from a wealthy family. Brilliant, ambitious, impatient.” Professor Li’s face darkened. “He wanted to use alchemy for profit. To mass-produce pills and sell them to the highest bidder. Your grandmother refused to teach him the final formulas.”

“So he left.”

“So he left. And he took everything she’d taught him—the foundations, the theory, the basic furnace techniques—and twisted them into something else.” He pulled out another photo. “This is him. Twenty years ago.”

Liang Yue looked at the image. A young man, maybe twenty-five, with sharp features and eyes that burned with cold intelligence. He stood beside Grandma in front of her old apothecary shop, both of them smiling.

Grandma had never mentioned him. Not once.

“What’s his name?”

“Chen Weimin. He changed it after the schism. Now he goes by Chen Feng.” Professor Li’s jaw tightened. “He’s the Zhang family’s head of research and development.”

The pieces clicked into place like a lock opening. Chen Feng—the disciple who turned. The Zhang family—the corporation funding artificial Qi. Grandma—the obstacle who had to be removed.

“They killed her for the formulas,” Liang Yue whispered. “But she gave me the furnace. She gave me everything.”

“She knew they’d come for her. That’s why she waited until the end to pass on the furnace. She wanted you to have it, but she also wanted to protect you for as long as possible.” Professor Li’s voice cracked. “I’m sorry, Liang Yue. I should have told you sooner. I should have—“

“You couldn’t have. I wasn’t ready.” Liang Yue looked at Grandma’s autopsy photos one more time. The glowing marker. The poisoned cells. The slow, agonizing death disguised as natural decline.

Her vision cleared. The furnace stopped blazing. Everything inside her went cold and still.

“I need to see him,” she said.

“Absolutely not.”

“I need to see Chen Feng. Face to face.”

“That’s suicide. The Zhang family has security everywhere. They have connections in every government office, every police precinct, every hospital. If you walk into their headquarters, you’ll never walk out.”

“Then I won’t walk in.” Liang Yue turned from the microscope. “I’ll make him come to me.”

Professor Li stared at her. “What are you planning?”

She didn’t answer. Instead, she reached into her bag and pulled out a small jade bottle—one of the few she’d kept from Grandma’s collection. Inside, a single pill glowed with faint golden light.

“This is the Rebirth Pill,” she said. “The last one Grandma ever made. It can bring someone back from the brink of death. Repair damaged organs. Reverse poisoning.”

“You can’t use that as bait. It’s priceless.”

“It’s the only thing Chen Feng wants that he can’t synthesize.” Liang Yue held up the bottle. “The Zhang family has money, labs, and a twisted version of alchemy. But they don’t have this. They can’t make it without the furnace, without Grandma’s bloodline, without the true Way.”

“If you reveal you have it, they’ll come for you immediately.”

“I’m counting on it.”

Professor Li’s face went pale. “You’re going to use yourself as bait.”

“I’m going to use the truth as bait.” Liang Yue tucked the bottle back into her bag. “Grandma didn’t die of old age. She was murdered. And the man who killed her is still out there, still poisoning people, still twisting the Way into something dark.”

Her phone buzzed. She glanced at the screen—a message from an unknown number.

*Miss Liang. We need to talk. Your grandmother’s death was not an accident. I can prove it. Meet me at the Jinsha Site Museum. Midnight. Come alone.*

Liang Yue’s blood ran cold.

“What is it?” Professor Li asked.

She showed him the message. His expression darkened. “This is a trap.”

“Probably.”

“You can’t go.”

“I have to.” Liang Yue’s fingers tightened on her phone. “Whoever sent this knows about Grandma. Knows about the poisoning. If they have evidence, I need to see it.”

“Or they’ll have a gun waiting for you.”

“Then I’ll have the furnace.” She met his eyes. “You said yourself—I’m the last Internal Alchemist. If I can’t defend myself against a single traitor, what kind of successor am I?”

Professor Li wanted to argue. She could see it in the way his jaw worked, the way his hands clenched and unclenched. But he also saw the steel in her spine, the fire behind her eyes.

“I’ll come with you,” he said finally.

“No. They said alone.”

“Then I’ll follow at a distance. If anything goes wrong, I’ll call for help.”

“Who would you call? The police? The Zhang family owns them, remember?”

He was silent. There was no good answer. No safe option. Only a choice between bad and worse.

Liang Yue looked at Grandma’s autopsy photos one last time. The poisoned cells. The dying stars.

“I’ll go,” she said. “And I’ll find out who sent that message. And then I’ll find Chen Feng.”

“And then what?”

She didn’t answer. Because the truth was, she didn’t know. She was a pharmacy student with a thousand-year-old furnace and a murder to solve. She had no allies, no resources, no plan beyond survival.

But she had Grandma’s blood in her veins. And she had the Way.

That would have to be enough.

---

The Jinsha Site Museum stood in the darkness like a sleeping beast. Its curved roofs and ancient architecture whispered of dynasties long dead, of gold masks and bronze vessels and rituals performed under starlight.

Liang Yue arrived at 11:47 PM. The gates were locked, but a side door stood slightly ajar, a sliver of light bleeding through the crack.

She pushed it open.

The museum’s interior was silent. Exhibition cases stood like glass coffins, each holding artifacts from the Shu civilization—jade discs, ivory carvings, bronze sun wheels. The air smelled of dust and preservation chemicals and something else.

Something metallic. Like blood.

Liang Yue walked forward, her footsteps echoing on the polished floor. Her Qi sense extended, probing the darkness. She felt no one ahead. No one behind.

But she felt something. A presence. Faint, like a whisper at the edge of hearing.

She rounded a corner and stopped.

A figure stood in the center of the main hall, backlit by a single spotlight. The sun wheel exhibit loomed behind them—a bronze disk with four radiating beams, the symbol of ancient Shu astronomy.

The figure turned.

It was a woman. Maybe forty, with sharp cheekbones and silver-streaked hair pulled back in a severe bun. She wore a black business suit that probably cost more than Liang Yue’s entire wardrobe.

“Miss Liang,” the woman said. Her voice was smooth, controlled. “Thank you for coming.”

“Who are you?”

“My name is Zhang Yulan.” She smiled, and it didn’t reach her eyes. “I’m the CEO of Zhang Pharmaceuticals. And I believe we have a common enemy.”

Liang Yue’s hand went to her bag. The furnace pulsed, ready.

“You’re a Zhang. Why should I trust anything you say?”

“Because Chen Feng killed my father too.” Zhang Yulan’s smile faded. “And I want him dead just as much as you do.”

title: The Poison in the Blood
content:
The chemical marker glowed like tiny stars against the dark tissue of Grandma's liver. Liang Yue had seen enough histology slides in her pharmacology classes to recognize heavy metal accumulation—but this was different. The pattern was too precise, too organized, like a signature written in cellular death.

"What am I looking at?" she asked, her voice steady despite the rage building in her chest.

Professor Li pointed to a cluster of dark spots. "Modified aconitum. Not the raw compound—that would have killed her in hours. This was refined, layered with other herbs to slow the absorption. It took six months to reach lethal concentration."

"Six months." Liang Yue's hands curled into fists. "Someone was poisoning her for six months, and no one noticed?"
