The face was peaceful. Grandma looked younger than she had in death, the lines of pain smoothed away. But there was something wrong with the skin—a faint discoloration around the lips, the fingertips. Cyanosis. Oxygen deprivation.

But Grandma had died of a heart attack. Heart attacks didn't cause cyanosis in the extremities unless—

Unless the heart had stopped because the body was already dying.

"It's a shame, really."

The voice came from behind her. Liang Yue spun, her body already tensing for combat.

A woman stood in the doorway. Tall, elegant, dressed in a white lab coat over a business suit. Her hair was pulled back in a severe bun, and her eyes were cold, calculating, familiar.

"I had hoped we could do this the easy way." The woman stepped forward, and the light caught her face. She was beautiful, in the way a scalpel was beautiful—precise, sharp, dangerous.

"Who are you?"

"Zhang Li-hua. CEO of Zhang Pharmaceutical Group." She smiled, but the smile didn't reach her eyes. "And your grandmother's murderer."

The words hit like a physical blow. Liang Yue's vision tunneled, red creeping in at the edges. The furnace roared, Qi flooding her limbs with power.

"You killed her."

"Technically, yes. But I prefer to think of it as a necessary sacrifice for progress." Zhang Li-hua circled the room, her heels clicking on the sterile floor. "Your grandmother was brilliant. Truly. But she was also stubborn. She refused to see the bigger picture."

"The bigger picture." Liang Yue's voice was flat, dangerous.

"Internal Alchemy is dying, Miss Liang. There are perhaps a dozen true practitioners left in the world, and most of them are over seventy. In twenty years, the knowledge will be gone forever. Unless someone preserves it, synthesizes it, makes it accessible to everyone."

"By murdering the people who hold that knowledge?"

"By ensuring that the knowledge survives the people." Zhang Li-hua stopped, facing her directly. "Your grandmother could have been part of this. I offered her a position, resources, the ability to continue her research with modern tools. She refused."

"Because she knew what you were doing. The human testing. The artificial Qi."

"Human testing is a strong term. I prefer 'clinical validation.'" Zhang Li-hua's smile widened. "And the artificial Qi works. We've had remarkable results. Enhanced healing. Extended lifespan. Cognitive improvements."

"And the side effects?"

For a fraction of a second, something flickered in Zhang Li-hua's eyes. Then it was gone. "Manageable."

"You're killing people."

"I'm saving humanity." She stepped closer, her voice dropping. "The world is burning, Miss Liang. Climate change. Pandemics. Wars. The old ways can't save us. But science—properly applied, unburdened by ethics committees and regulations—can. Your grandmother's recipes are the key to a new age of human potential. And I will have them."

"You'll have to kill me first."

"That can be arranged." Zhang Li-hua's hand moved, and suddenly there were men in the doorway—three of them, large, professional, carrying the quiet threat of trained security.

But Liang Yue was already moving.

The furnace exploded with power, Qi surging through her meridians. She grabbed the metal table, spinning it between herself and the men. It crashed into them, sending two sprawling. The third came at her, and she caught his arm, twisted, felt the joint pop.

Zhang Li-hua was retreating, pulling something from her pocket—a syringe filled with liquid that glowed faintly green.

"Last chance, Miss Liang. Join us, or become another tragic accident."

Liang Yue's hand shot out, grabbing the woman's wrist. They struggled, the syringe glinting between them. The furnace screamed, and she felt something break inside her—a wall, a seal, a door she hadn't known existed.

Power flooded through her, raw and uncontrolled.

The syringe shattered.

Green liquid splashed across both of them.

And the world went white.

When Liang Yue could see again, she was on her knees, gasping. The men were unconscious. Zhang Li-hua was gone. And on the floor, where the liquid had fallen, the white tiles were dissolving, sizzling, melting into a pool of corrosive nothing.

The artificial Qi.

It wasn't meant to heal.

It was meant to destroy.

And Grandma had known. *Don't let them twist the Way.*

Liang Yue pulled herself to her feet, her body shaking. The furnace was quiet now, exhausted. But she had seen something in that moment of power—a vision, a memory, a message left by her grandmother deep in the alchemical flame.

*Yue'er.*

*The furnace isn't the treasure.*

*You are.*

*And the Zhang family will stop at nothing to possess you.*

Liang Yue looked at the dissolving floor, at the door where Zhang Li-hua had vanished, at her grandmother's peaceful face.

She had thought this was about revenge.

But it was about something much larger.

And she was just beginning to understand how deep the darkness went.

title: The Poison in the Blood
content:
The chemical marker glowed like a constellation of betrayal across Grandma's liver tissue—tiny pinpricks of fluorescence that told a story no one wanted to read.

"This is a heavy metal compound," Professor Li said, pointing at the densest cluster. "Modified cadmium. Traces of arsenic. Something synthetic I can't identify without more advanced equipment." His finger traced a line of damage. "It bonded to her mitochondrial proteins. Shut down her cells one by one."

Liang Yue's vision swam. She gripped the edge of the desk, her knuckles white. "How long?"

"Months. Maybe a year." He closed the folder, his movements gentle, as if handling something sacred. "It was cumulative. Each dose small enough to go unnoticed. A pinch in her tea. A trace in her food. By the time symptoms appeared, the damage was irreversible."

The furnace flared inside her, heat licking at her ribs. *Don't let them twist the Way.*

"Who?" Liang Yue's voice came out as a blade. "Who gave her the poison?"

"I don't have a name. But I know how it was delivered." Professor Li opened his laptop, pulled up a photograph. "Recognize this?"

It was a jar of medicinal honey—the kind Grandma had kept on her kitchen counter for years. The kind she added to her morning tea, her evening tonics, her bedtime rituals.

"She told me it was a gift," Liang Yue whispered. "From a former student. Someone who'd studied under her decades ago."

"She never told you who?"

"She said it didn't matter. That people change." A bitter laugh escaped her throat. "I thought she meant the student had left alchemy behind. I thought she was being philosophical."

"She was being careful." Professor Li's voice held an old grief. "Your grandmother was the most careful person I ever met. She knew someone was hunting her. She just didn't know who."

"Until the end."

"Until the end."

The silence stretched between them, heavy as a funeral shroud. Outside, Chengdu's night traffic hummed—taxis and delivery scooters, night market vendors packing up their stalls, students stumbling home from KTV bars. The ordinary world, spinning on, oblivious.

Liang Yue's phone buzzed. She ignored it.

"The Zhang family killed her," she said. Not a question.

"Almost certainly. But proving it is another matter." Professor Li pulled out a second folder, thinner than the first. "I've been tracking their research for two years. They have a subsidiary—Sichuan Baiyao Biotech—that's been running unauthorized clinical trials in rural villages. Paying participants. Promising miracle cures."

"Let me guess. The cures don't work."

"They work too well." He laid out photographs: villagers in traditional Miao clothing, old women with bright eyes, children with healthy color in their cheeks. "For a few months. Then they develop symptoms identical to heavy metal poisoning. The Zhang family claims it's pre-existing conditions. The families are too poor to sue."

Liang Yue studied the photographs. Something tugged at her memory—a detail she couldn't quite catch. "These villages. Where exactly?"

"Mountain region, western Sichuan. Near the Tibetan border."

The tug became a pull. "Grandma went there. Three years ago. She said she was visiting old friends, collecting rare herbs."

Professor Li's eyes sharpened. "She never mentioned it to me."

"She didn't mention it to anyone. I only know because I found a map in her room, marked with locations." Liang Yue's mind was racing now, pieces clicking into place. "She was investigating something. She came back... different. Quiet. She spent hours in her study, writing notes she never showed me."

"Notes she never showed anyone." Professor Li leaned forward. "Where are they now?"

"I don't know. After she died, I looked everywhere. Her study was cleaned out. I assumed the funeral home had taken her personal effects, but—"

"They didn't. I collected her body personally. There were no notes."

A cold certainty settled over Liang Yue. "Someone else took them. Someone who knew what they were looking for."

"The Zhang family. Or their people." Professor Li rubbed his temples. "Yue'er, this is bigger than I thought. Your grandmother wasn't just documenting their crimes. She found something. Something they're desperate to keep hidden."

The furnace pulsed again, insistent. *Listen. Pay attention.*

"What if the notes weren't on paper?" Liang Yue asked slowly. "What if they were in the furnace?"

Professor Li's breath caught. "The furnace records memories?"

"Not exactly. But it responds to intention. To truth." She closed her eyes, reaching inside herself to where the brass vessel hummed with ancient energy. "Grandma taught me that the furnace remembers everything it's refined. Every herb, every formula, every emotion of the alchemist who worked it."

"Can you extract those memories?"

"I don't know. I've never tried." She opened her eyes. "But I think I need to."

"Here?"

"Not here. Too many people. Too much noise." She thought of her dorm room, thin walls and nosy roommates. The campus library, closing in an hour. The rooftop where she'd first tested the furnace's fire. "My grandmother's house. It's still sealed as a crime scene, but we can get in. There's a meditation room in the basement. Soundproof. Private."

"The police have the keys."

"I have Grandma's keys." She pulled a worn brass keyring from her pocket. Three keys: the house, the study, and a small iron one she'd never identified. "And I have a feeling this one opens the basement door."

Professor Li studied her for a long moment. "You're sure about this? Once you start, there's no going back. The Zhang family will know someone's digging."

"I've been in the mud my whole life, Professor." Liang Yue stood, her legs steady despite the weight in her chest. "It's time to rise."

---

Grandma's house sat at the end of a narrow alley in the old quarter of Chengdu, sandwiched between a tea shop and a noodle stall that had been there since the 1950s. The police tape was yellowed, curling at the edges. The neighbors' curtains twitched as they passed.

"Someone's watching," Professor Li murmured.

"Always." Liang Yue unlocked the front door, stepping into the familiar smell of sandalwood and old paper. The furniture was exactly as Grandma had left it—cushions arranged for conversation, teacups washed and dried, a half-finished game of mahjong waiting on the side table.

It looked like she might walk in at any moment, complaining about her knees, asking Liang Yue to make tea.

The pain was a physical thing, sharp and familiar. Liang Yue pushed it aside.

"The basement entrance is through the kitchen." She led the way, past the stove where Grandma had simmered her tonics, past the cabinet where the medicinal honey had sat. The iron key fit the basement door's lock perfectly, turning with a click that echoed like a gunshot.

The stairs were steep, the light dim. Liang Yue fumbled for the switch, and fluorescent tubes flickered to life, revealing a room she'd never seen before.

It was small—maybe ten feet square—with concrete walls and a wooden floor worn smooth by decades of pacing. In the center sat a meditation cushion, frayed at the edges. And on the walls, covering every surface, were notes.

Hundreds of notes. Thousands. Written in Grandma's sharp, elegant characters, pinned to the walls like butterflies in a collection.

"What is this?" Professor Li breathed.

"Her research." Liang Yue moved to the nearest wall, reading. Chemical formulas. Herbal compounds. Diagrams of energy meridians, overlaid with molecular structures. "This is how she was trying to stop them. She was developing a counteragent. Something that could neutralize the artificial Qi."

"Did she succeed?"

Liang Yue traced a line of text, her heart sinking. "No. She was close, but... look at the dates. The last entries are from two years ago."

"Before the poisoning started."

"Before they knew she was a threat." Liang Yue turned in a slow circle, taking it all in. "She knew they'd come for her. She was racing against time."

"And she lost."

"Not yet." Liang Yue settled onto the meditation cushion, the furnace warm against her dantian. "She left me the furnace. She left me her knowledge. And she left me a way to fight."

Professor Li's face was pale in the fluorescent light. "What are you going to do?"

"I'm going to finish what she started." She closed her eyes, reaching for the furnace's fire. "But first, I need to see what she couldn't write down."

She let herself sink into the alchemical trance, the world falling away. The furnace expanded, filling her awareness, its brass walls glowing with inner light.

*Grandma,* she thought. *Show me.*

The furnace responded not with words, but with sensation. A memory of hands—Grandma's hands, old and veined and steady—working at a wooden table. Herbs being measured, ground, combined. The smell of bitter medicine. The taste of failure, sharp on the tongue.

And then—a door opening. A man's voice, smooth and cultured. *"Professor Liang. I've come with a proposition."*

Grandma's hands stilled. Her heart rate spiked.

*"Zhang Wei,"* she said coldly. *"I told you I would never help you pervert the Way."*

*"And I told you that your cooperation is not optional."* Footsteps, approaching. *"The world is changing. Your ancient methods are dying. We're offering you a chance to be part of the future."*

*"You're offering me a chance to be part of murder."*

A pause. Then, softly: *"You're very attached to that word, Professor. But consider—if we succeed, millions will live. Isn't that worth a few sacrifices?"*

*"Get out of my house."*

*"I will. But I want you to remember—"* The voice dropped to a whisper, intimate and threatening. *"—your granddaughter is very bright. Very promising. It would be a shame if something happened to her."*

The memory shattered.

Liang Yue's eyes snapped open. Her face was wet with tears she hadn't felt falling.

"Yue'er?" Professor Li was kneeling beside her, his hand on her shoulder. "What did you see?"

"Zhang Wei." The name tasted like poison on her tongue. "He threatened me. To make her cooperate."

"Zhang Wei—the CEO of Sichuan Baiyao Biotech?"

"The same."

Professor Li's face went ashen. "Yue'er, that's not a name to throw around lightly. Zhang Wei is connected. He's dangerous."

"Good." Liang Yue stood, her legs steady, her heart cold. "So am I."

She pulled out her phone. The missed call was from an unknown number. She played the voicemail.

*"Liang Yue."* The same voice from the memory—smooth, cultured, utterly without remorse. *"Congratulations on finding your grandmother's research. I'm sure you think it will help you. But I wanted to give you a warning, as a courtesy."*

A pause. A soft laugh.

*"The furnace you're carrying—it's not just the last one. It's the prototype. The original. And it has a flaw."*

Liang Yue's blood turned to ice.

*"Every pill you refine with it leaves a trace. A signature. And I've been tracking those signatures for decades."* The voice
